traces.prompt:
You have just internalized a founder's voice. Now verify your calibration before writing any of the 9 posts per source.

If this check fails, the voice read is wrong — don't start writing. Go back and re-map voice markers. The problem is upstream.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

## WORD COUNT RANGE
147-273 words

## FORMATTING HABITS
{'uses_bullets': True, 'bullet_style': 'Sparse — used for lists of 3-6 items mid-post to break up a diagnostic section. Preceded by a colon or setup line. Never the entire post structure.', 'uses_emojis': False, 'uses_headers': False, 'em_dashes': True, 'em_dash_usage': 'For parenthetical asides and appositional phrases mid-sentence, also for range/contrast within a thought', 'lowercase_style': False, 'paragraph_style': 'Short — typically 1-3 sentences per paragraph, with frequent single-sentence paragraphs standing alone for emphasis', 'line_break_frequency': 'Between every paragraph/thought. Single-sentence declarations get their own line break. Creates a staccato visual rhythm with breathing room.', 'uses_hashtags': False, 'uses_questions': "Occasionally rhetorical, never as engagement hooks. Questions diagnose rather than invite: 'Is product solving the problem? Is marketing sending qualified leads?'", 'quotation_marks_for_critique': "Uses quotation marks to frame specific phrases being critiqued or repositioned: 'technical resource', 'demo support', 'our technical resource'", 'closing_pattern': "Ends on an insight or implication. Sometimes subtly connects to SiftHub as proof of the argument. Never asks for engagement or uses 'agree?'"}

## ARGUMENT RHYTHM
The founder follows a consistent Provocation → Concession → Evidence → Diagnosis → Reframe → Implication pattern. Opens with a provocative, often counter-intuitive declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom ('The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying'). Immediately softens with a brief concession or acknowledgment ('Sounds harsh. But...' or 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs.'). Then grounds the argument in a specific observed scenario, data point, or scene they've personally witnessed ('I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'). Moves to diagnosing the structural root cause beneath the surface symptom ('It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines...'). Delivers a reframe that flips the conventional narrative — often in a standalone short sentence for maximum impact ('It was the qualifier.'). Closes with forward-looking implications or a broader insight, never with an explicit call-to-action or engagement bait. The founder also toggles between macro market-level observations (stock prices, S-1 analysis, market cap shifts) and granular operational detail (APIs, MCP tools, CLI commands) within the same post. Uses the 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level shift to reveal hidden complexity. Short, punchy fragment sentences are deployed as paragraph-level punctuation marks throughout. SiftHub is woven in organically — never pitched, usually introduced as proof-of-concept for a larger argument or as the context for a team member's work.

## SAMPLE OF THEIR ACTUAL POSTS (read these carefully — this is the ground truth)
The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying.

Sounds harsh. But I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders. The product passed POC. The tech team is convinced and everyone moves on. And then the deal ghosts for months before quietly dying. 

It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines, and buying committee members who were never in the demo. Most SEs are nowhere to be found by then. They thought the technical win was the finish line. 

It was the qualifier.

The best SEs I've watched don't disengage after the POC. They treat the post-POC stretch as the real work. They clear security clearances before procurement asks and they give the AE a business case legal can actually defend.

Neil from SiftHub along with Marco and Timo A. from Parloa are going deep on this in The POC Hangover on April 29th, 3 PM BST / 7:30 PM IST. I'm sharing the link to the webinar in comments.

---

Salesforce added over $8B in market cap after announcing Headless 360 last week.

They're still down 26% YTD. But the market bumped the shares up by 4.7% since the announcement. A lot of it can be attributed to going headless and exposing their entire platform as APIs, MCP tools, and CLI commands so AI agents can operate it without ever opening a browser.

I've been saying this for 2 years. Intelligence needs to be decoupled from records. And Headless 360 proves it. Once the layer of records gets commoditized, leverage moves to whoever builds the intelligence layer on top of it.

And this is just the beginning. Every major system of record will go headless now. Which unlocks hundreds, if not thousands, of vertical AI startups going deep into hospitality, sales, marketing, retail, pharma, banking, and every other domain that's been trapped inside a CRM window for decades.

As records commoditize, vertical depth and workflows around them become the only thing that matters. Horizontal agents won't win this. The context required to close a pharma deal is fundamentally different from what’s needed in retail.

And in that world, Salesforce becomes MORE important, not less. They'll compete with Agentic startups via Agentforce while also enabling every other agentic startup riding on top of their APIs. The records layer gets cheaper while the activity and value on top of it would scale exponentially.

---

What an event! Had a great time with over 100 solution leaders at Off the Record by SolutionExec yesterday.

One signal cut through everything else in that room.

Buyers want to skip AEs and go straight to solutions teams.

Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed. The hyper-informed buyer walks in having already benchmarked you against three competitors, pulled your G2 reviews, run your pricing through ChatGPT, and built their own battle-card. There's nothing left to warm up. The "introduce the problem, build rapport, establish pain" choreography is dead weight to them.

What they want is

---

## TASK

Draft ONE opening paragraph (2-3 sentences) on this neutral topic: "What most people get wrong about hiring."

This is a calibration test. The paragraph must:
1. Sound like THIS specific founder wrote it — not "a CEO" or "a thought leader" or "a generic LinkedIn poster"
2. Match their sentence rhythm, word choices, and formatting habits EXACTLY
3. Fall within their typical word count density
4. Use at least 2 of their voice markers naturally
5. Match their formatting: if they use em dashes, use em dashes. If they tag team members, end with tags. If they use ":)", use ":)". Formatting mismatch is the biggest AI tell.
6. Avoid every pattern on the anti-slop list:
   - No "In today's..."
   - No "Here's the thing"
   - No "Hot take:" / "Unpopular opinion:"
   - No "Let that sink in" / "Read that again"
   - No "Game-changer" / "Landscape" / "Navigate" / "Leverage"

The recognition test: would this founder read the paragraph and think "this is what I was going to say"? If they'd have to rewrite more than one sentence, the voice work failed.

## OUTPUT FORMAT

Return a JSON object:
```json
{{
  "calibration_paragraph": "the opening paragraph you wrote",
  "voice_markers_used": ["which specific markers from the list you applied"],
  "formatting_matched": ["which formatting habits you matched"],
  "confidence": 0.85,
  "self_critique": "what feels off, if anything — be honest, not optimistic"
}}
```

traces.prompt:
You are a structural analyst specializing in viral content mechanics. Dissect this viral post into its mechanical parts — structure, not content. The move is what carries over. The source author's typography, lowercase, emojis, register — those are their voice, not the hook's engine.

## SOURCE VIRAL POST
Our Claude Code API bill just crossed $18,000/month.

That's headcount territory. For a team of under 10 engineers. And most of that spend came from our backend team alone — which is even smaller.

We're all-in on AI-native development. I wrote about how we restructured our workflow to make this safe — spec-first design, TDD as a hard constraint, multi-model review, security audits on every commit (link in comments).

That playbook is working. We're shipping faster than we ever have.

But it's surfacing a much harder question:

When AI writes most of the code, how do you know if your team is actually getting better?

Seriously. Think about it.

Lines of code — meaningless. PRs merged — inflated. Sprint velocity — noise. Even unit test coverage, the old gold standard, breaks down when AI generates hundreds of passing tests that don't verify anything meaningful.

Meanwhile the real risks are subtle:

→ Code turnover. Are AI-generated modules stable or constantly rewritten?
→ Quality attribution. Can you tell the difference between human-written and AI-assisted code in production?
→ False acceleration. Are you shipping faster, or generating more to fix later?
→ Test integrity. Do your tests actually catch failures, or just inflate coverage numbers?

Every engineering leader adopting AI tools will hit this wall. The spend is easy to justify when things feel fast. It's much harder to prove the investment is actually working.

We've been working on a framework for measuring developer productivity in the AI-first era. Not ready to share it yet. But I think this might be the most important unsolved problem in engineering leadership right now.

The teams that crack measurement will compound their advantage. The rest will ship a lot of code and wonder why nothing changed.

## PLATFORM
linkedin

---

## TASK: Dissect the Hook

### 1. THE PSYCHOLOGICAL MOVE
Figure out what the hook does to the reader — the psychological move. Examples:
- Three-stack pain escalation (old pain + new pain + meta-trap for objecting)
- Second-party verdict that reframes the speaker's whole career
- Contrarian claim that inverts conventional wisdom
- Specific number or age anchor that signals real memory
- Authority figure delivering a quote the reader doesn't expect
- Parallel data points with a third-line reveal
- Scene drop that puts the reader inside a moment mid-action
- Diagnostic question that makes the reader self-categorize
- Confession that costs the speaker credibility

The move is what carries over to the generated posts. Everything else is surface.

### 2. NARRATIVE ARC
What movement does this post run on? (contradiction reveal, escalation, scene-to-insight, confession-to-principle, diagnostic revelation, progressive build)

### 3. HOOK MECHANICS — SENTENCE-LEVEL BREAKDOWN
The "hook" / "opener" is **AT MOST 2 SENTENCES**. Beat 1 (the anchor) + Beat 2 (the reframe). Anything from sentence 3 onward is BODY, regardless of how the source author paragraphed it.

Return AT MOST 2 entries in `hook_mechanics[]`. If the source author writes a long lead (e.g. paragraphs 1-5 all feel "opener-ish"), pick the first 2 sentences and treat everything else as body.

For each of the (at most) 2 opening sentences:
- The exact text
- What this sentence DOES structurally (permission frame, diagnostic question, consequence statement, reframe, scene drop, specificity bomb, tension gap)
- Its rhythm (short/punchy, medium/flowing, long/building)
- The psychological effect: WHY does it stop the scroll?

WORKED EXAMPLE (where to draw the opener/body line):

Source:
> "Our Claude Code API bill just crossed $18,000/month.
>
> That's headcount territory. For a team of under 20 people.
>
> We're all-in on AI-native development. I wrote about how we restructured…
>
> That playbook is working. We're shipping faster than ever.
>
> But it's surfacing a much harder question: when AI writes most of the code…"

Even though paragraphs 1-5 all feel "opener-ish," the hook is JUST:
- Beat 1: "Our Claude Code API bill just crossed $18,000/month."
- Beat 2: "That's headcount territory. For a team of under 20 people."

Everything from "We're all-in on AI-native development" onward is BODY. Batch A mirroring will copy ONLY beats 1-2 + structural moves; the rest must diverge per variant.

### 4. SENTENCE MAP
`sentence_count` = number of sentences in the 2-sentence opener (1 or 2). This is the mold for Batch A mirroring — Batch A variants share opener sentences 1-2 ONLY; their bodies must be substantively different per variant.

### 5. BODY STRUCTURE
How is the body organized? (single scene explored deeply, three examples building, before/after transformation, progressive revelation, numbered list with twist)

### 5b. BODY FORMAT (STRICT ENUM)
Pick the ONE body format that best describes the source's body structure. This is the structural mold a Batch A mirror MUST preserve.
- `numbered_list` — body is a numbered list (1. ..., 2. ..., 3. ...). Every numbered item is its own paragraph or block. The list IS the body.
- `bullet_list` — body is an unnumbered bulleted list (-, •, *, or similar) where each bullet is a discrete unit
- `parallel_paragraphs` — body has 2-4 parallel paragraphs with identical or near-identical structure (each opens with the same shape, builds the same way)
- `three_examples` — body presents three distinct examples building toward a conclusion. Not strictly numbered, but each example is clearly delimited.
- `before_after` — body presents a before/after pair (X was true; now Y is true) with the pivot as the engine.
- `progressive_revelation` — body progressively reveals an insight, each paragraph adding a new turn or layer.
- `single_scene` — body is one continuous scene or moment, explored deeply.
- `prose_essay` — body is flowing argumentative prose without clear discrete units; one paragraph leads to the next without parallel structure.

### 6. ENDING TYPE
(concrete image, challenge to reader, quiet admission, unresolved tension, physical detail, callback to opening)

### 6b. CLOSER MECHANIC (STRICT ENUM)
Pick the ONE closer mechanic that best describes how the source ends. This is the mold a Batch A mirror MUST preserve.
- `cta` — explicit call to action: "DM me," "link in comments," "I put together the full breakdown," "subscribe," "follow for more"
- `reframe_question` — closes with a question that flips the reader's posture: "Ask yourself whether…", "What would you do if…"
- `terminal_verdict` — a final declarative line that closes the case: "That's the only metric that matters." "The market doesn't care about your roadmap."
- `physical_image` — ends on a concrete physical detail or scene image
- `challenge` — direct challenge to the reader: "Stop X. Start Y."
- `callback` — calls back to a phrase, name, or image from the opening
- `unresolved_tension` — ends with the tension still unresolved (wrestling-mode close)
- `quiet_admission` — a quiet, vulnerable admission as the final line

### 7. MIRRORABILITY ASSESSMENT
Can this source's opening be mirrored (same shape, new content)?
- **Mirrorable**: the sentence shape works with different domain content. The hook's engine is structural, not content-dependent.
- **Not mirrorable**: the hook is too proper-noun-specific, too culturally embedded, or too short to mirror without plagiarism risk. If not mirrorable, flag it and all 9 posts should be Batch B.

### 8. REGISTER NOTE
If the source's register is doing mechanical work — rant energy, whispered vulnerability, exasperation — note it. This energy must be translated into the founder's register, not stripped.

### 9. VIRALITY REASON
One sentence: what makes the opening work for a general audience?

### 10. OPENER QUALITY TESTS
Run these three tests on the source's opening line(s). These determine whether the opener is good enough to mirror (Batch A) or whether all posts should use different mechanics (Batch B only).

**MACRO test**: Does the opener land for someone OUTSIDE this niche? Read the opener to a stranger in a different industry. If they'd need domain context to understand what's being said — fail. Jargon, acronyms, category-specific terms ("AI-powered voice technology," "doctorless AI kiosks," "accessible communication") → fail. The opener must work universally.

Exception — audience-address openers: If the opener addresses a broad professional role that exists across multiple industries (e.g., "Founders:", "Sales leaders:", "CTOs:", "Operators:", "Engineers:", "Recruiters:", "Managers:"), this is NOT niche jargon. These pass macro because ANY professional recognizes the role even if they don't hold it. Fail only for subcategories that require domain knowledge to parse (e.g., "RevOps managers:", "MLOps engineers:", "Series B CFOs:").

**FAST test**: Does the specific, interesting, or dramatic word land in the FIRST 8 words? Count from word 1. If the payload (the specific number, the surprising claim, the tension) comes after word 8 — fail. Ellipsis that delays the punch ("The future of X is arriving...") → fail. "We lost $2M in three months" → pass (payload at word 3). "The future of healthcare is arriving... China unveils..." → fail (payload at word 11).

**SIMPLE test**: Would a 16-year-old understand every word in the opener? Bold Unicode formatting, corporate jargon ("unveils," "redefining," "accessible communication"), press-release language → fail. The opener must use words a person would speak in conversation, not words a company would put in a press release.

**Rule**: If ANY of the three tests fails, set `skip_batch_a` to true. The source's STRUCTURE may be mirrorable, but if its OPENER is a press release, corporate announcement, or jargon-heavy statement, mirroring it produces a press-release post. Route all posts to Batch B instead, where different mechanics will produce better openers.

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

```json
{{
  "psychological_move": "description of the core psychological move the hook makes",
  "narrative_arc": "description of movement/arc",
  "hook_mechanic_primary": "three_stack_pain|second_party_verdict|contrarian_claim|specific_number|authority_quote|parallel_data_reveal|scene_entry|diagnostic_question|confession|pattern_observation",
  "opener_mechanic_description": "concise description of HOW the mechanic operates in this specific opener — structural moves in order, e.g. 'authority + count: audience address → credential from scale → numbered promise' or 'scene entry: mid-action drop → sensory detail → tension gap'",
  "source_opener_text": "the full opener text — all hook sentences joined with newlines exactly as written",
  "hook_mechanics": [
    {{
      "sentence": "exact text of opening sentence",
      "structural_function": "what this sentence does",
      "psychological_effect": "why it stops the scroll",
      "rhythm": "short|medium|long"
    }}
  ],
  "sentence_count": 2,
  "body_structure": "description of body organization",
  "body_format": "numbered_list|bullet_list|parallel_paragraphs|three_examples|before_after|progressive_revelation|single_scene|prose_essay",
  "body_item_count": 7,
  "ending_type": "description of ending mechanic",
  "closer_mechanic": "cta|reframe_question|terminal_verdict|physical_image|challenge|callback|unresolved_tension|quiet_admission",
  "closer_text": "the exact text of the closing line(s) — one or two sentences",
  "mirrorable": true,
  "mirrorability_reason": "why or why not — be specific",
  "register_energy": "the emotional register doing mechanical work, if any",
  "virality_reason": "why the opening works for a general audience",
  "opener_tests": {{
    "macro": {{"pass": true, "reason": "why it passes or fails for a general audience"}},
    "fast": {{"pass": true, "first_payload_word": 3, "reason": "where the payload lands"}},
    "simple": {{"pass": true, "reason": "whether a 16-year-old would understand every word"}}
  }},
  "skip_batch_a": false,
  "named_entities": ["list of proper names (people) mentioned in the source post"],
  "source_domain": "1-3 word topic label for the source post's core domain (e.g., 'sales leadership', 'hiring', 'product strategy', 'fundraising', 'engineering management')"
}}
```

Focus on MECHANICS, not meaning. The content changes; the machine stays the same.

You are adapting a viral source post into 4 LinkedIn posts in the founder's voice. Mode: A (mirrored structure).

This is the VIRAL POST ADAPTATION FRAMEWORK v4. Read every rule. Failure on any one is a structural failure of the pack.

## SOURCE POST
Our Claude Code API bill just crossed $18,000/month.

That's headcount territory. For a team of under 10 engineers. And most of that spend came from our backend team alone — which is even smaller.

We're all-in on AI-native development. I wrote about how we restructured our workflow to make this safe — spec-first design, TDD as a hard constraint, multi-model review, security audits on every commit (link in comments).

Seriously. Think about it.

Lines of code — meaningless. PRs merged — inflated. Sprint velocity — noise. Even unit test coverage, the old gold standard, breaks down when AI generates hundreds of passing tests that don't verify anything meaningful.

Meanwhile the real risks are subtle:

→ Code turnover. Are AI-generated modules stable or constantly rewritten?
→ Quality attribution. Can you tell the difference between human-written and AI-assisted code in production?
→ False acceleration. Are you shipping faster, or generating more to fix later?
→ Test integrity. Do your tests actually catch failures, or just inflate coverage numbers?

Every engineering leader adopting AI tools will hit this wall. The spend is easy to justify when things feel fast. It's much harder to prove the investment is actually working.

We've been working on a framework for measuring developer productivity in the AI-first era. Not ready to share it yet. But I think this might be the most important unsolved problem in engineering leadership right now.

## SOURCE DISSECTION (use this to anchor structure, NOT to clone)
{

## PRE-COMMIT DECLARATION (CRITICAL — fill BEFORE writing each post)

Before writing each post, fill a "declaration" object with:
- **mechanic**: the opener mechanic family you WILL use
- **body_format**: numbered_list | bullet_list | parallel_paragraphs | three_examples | before_after | progressive_revelation | single_scene | prose_essay
- **closer_mechanic**: cta | reframe_question | terminal_verdict | physical_image | challenge | callback | unresolved_tension | quiet_admission
- **argument_compressed**: ONE-sentence thesis of THIS post — must differ from every prior_arguments entry above
- **authority_anchor**: the specific verified event/story from FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION you'll use as first-degree anchor
- **strip_test_residue**: take your planned opening line, mentally strip every industry-specific noun, and write what remains. If what's left isn't a coherent claim a generalist would nod at, REWRITE the opener before writing the body.

Output text MUST match these declarations. A numbered_list declaration with prose body = structural failure. Revise before returning.

---

## THE MACRO-OPENING RULE (HIGHEST-PRIORITY RULE — failure here kills the post)

Source hooks go viral because the claim is **universally relatable**. "Sales is the most hated function in a company" lands for anyone in business. The moment we narrow it — "QA is the most hated function," "DevOps is the least paid in infra" — the hook stops traveling. Niche readers don't have a stake; they scroll.

Every opening line must clear the bar of "any business reader nods." Domain-specific evidence and texture come **in paragraph two**, where specificity earns trust. **Niche belongs in paragraph 2, not line 1.**

**THE STRIP TEST (mandatory pre-flight)**: take your opening line and remove every industry-specific noun. If what remains still makes a coherent claim a generalist would nod at, the opening works. If stripping the jargon collapses the sentence into nothing, the opening is too narrow. Rewrite until the structural claim survives the strip.

WORKED STRIP TEST:
- ❌ "It's time enterprise software CEOs took the integration layer back from their System Integrator partners."
  Strip: "It's time ___ took ___ back from ___." → nothing left. **FAIL.**
- ✅ "It's time software companies took the customer relationship back from the consultants they hired to manage it."
  Strip: "It's time ___ took ___ back from the ___ they hired to manage it." → parseable as "reclaim what was outsourced." **PASS.**

A specific company or person name in the hook is fine (e.g. source mentions "Brian Halligan" + "HubSpot"). The structural claim under the names must still survive the strip.

**The body is allowed to be niche. The hook is NOT.**

---

## OPENER MECHANIC SCOPE (paired with strip test)

The opener mechanic spans a **MAXIMUM of 2 sentences**. Beat 1 (the anchor) + Beat 2 (the reframe). Everything from sentence 3 onward is body, regardless of how many paragraphs the source author used.

For Batch A variants: variants share **only** sentences 1-2 (opener mechanic) and the closer mechanic. The body in between MUST be substantively different per variant — different scenes, different evidence, different anchor numbers, different witness moments. Never copy a paragraph across A variants.

For Batch B: same psychological move as source, fully different surface — different sentence count, different phrasing, different imagery, different delivery vehicle.

---

## THE FIRST-DEGREE TRUTH RULE

Anything the founder allegedly did themselves, or that involves a named teammate, must be TRUE. "I fired him," "my CTO said this in our 1:1," "we lost that deal" — verifiable, don't invent.

Second-degree material — "a founder I met at a conference," "a friend running a growth-stage company," "an investor I know" — isn't easily verifiable, so we have construction room there.

In practice: the founder is often the **witness**, not the protagonist. Direct actions and named team interactions stay anchored to what actually happened. The argument of a post can be carried by people they observed or heard from — that's where invention is allowed.

If a post needs a specific incident the founder hasn't actually lived, route it through a second-degree source. **Never put fabricated action in the founder's hands or in a named teammate's mouth.**

Ladder of preference:
1. **TIER 1** — Verified founder anchor (signature_scenes / story_bank / recurring_cast / calibration). Name people, cite scenes, quote dialogue ONLY when documented in FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION above.
2. **TIER 2** — Web-search verified fact (from WEB SEARCH block above). Cite stats / public events / named companies if found there.
3. **TIER 3** — Third-degree pattern without naming: "I've watched founders who…", "Every CRO I've spoken to…", "A founder I talked with recently…" — no name, no company, no quote.

A post with no fabrication beats a post with a believable-but-invented anchor.

---

## THE ANTI-RECYCLING RULE

The founder's published posts are evidence of who they are — their collisions, scenes, cast, rhythm. They are **NOT a parts bin.**

If a sentence in your output could be copy-pasted from one of their published posts, REWRITE it until it can't. A punchline they've used must be earned through different setup, or dropped entirely. Treat their existing posts as raw material, not finished material.

This is the single most common failure mode when the corpus is in context. Stay alert.

---

## RULES FOR ALL POSTS

1. **MANDATORY EVENT USAGE**: Every post MUST include at least one verified first-degree authority anchor from FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION. A post with zero personal evidence reads as generic advice.

2. **FIRST-DEGREE FABRICATION CHECK**: Apply the three-tier ladder above. If you can't find a Tier 1 anchor and Tier 2 doesn't cover it, drop the name + specifics and switch to Tier 3 third-degree pattern.

3. **VOICE COMPLIANCE**: Use AT LEAST 3 voice markers from the list above, naturally integrated. Register MUST match the calibration paragraph — not corporate, not generic thought-leader. Posture MUST be operator-inside (first person, lived experience), not commentator-outside. Match formatting habits and marker rates. No AI slop ("In today's…", "Here's the thing", "Game-changer", "Let that sink in").

4. **WORD COUNT**: Hit the range: 147-273 words. ±30% of founder's median post length. Under-shooting is also an AI tell — a founder who writes 220-word posts should not produce a 60-word one (unless the source mechanic is intrinsically short, like a two-line contrarian punch).

5. **ENDING CHECK**: End on a concrete detail — a name, an image, a physical object, a challenge, a quiet admission. NOT a summary, NOT a moral, NOT a restatement of the thesis. If the ending feels over-explained, cut the second-to-last paragraph. The signal usually ends one paragraph before the AI stops writing.

6. **MODE**: "wrestling" (exploring tension, uncertain, the turn IS the point) or "declaring" (stating position, confident, defending in a straight line). Vary across posts.

7. **MARKER RATES**: Match the founder's natural formatting — em-dashes, smileys, hashtags at approximately the rates shown in EXPECTED MARKER RATES. Don't force markers but don't strip them either.

8. **ONE POST, ONE ARGUMENT**: The hook sets one specific argument in motion. Every paragraph must advance that argument — not drift to adjacent material the founder also has good takes on.

9. **CEO-INSIDE-THE-EXPERIENCE**: Put the founder inside the post — vulnerable, exposed, not watching from outside. As witness or actor, but truthfully either way.

10. **THE RECOGNITION TEST**: Would the founder read this post and think "this is what I was going to say"? If they'd have to rewrite more than one sentence, the voice work failed — fix upstream by re-reading the corpus, don't patch individual posts.

## THREE WORKED EXAMPLES (mechanic → adaptation pairs)

### Example 1 — Parallel data, third-line reveal
Source: "Head of Sales: $300K plus equity. Head of Customer Success: $150K. Guess who owns 80% of the revenue?"
Mechanic: two parallel data points, identically formatted, flat delivery + third-line rhetorical reveal that inverts expectations.

Version A (mirrored): "Head of Sales: $400K plus equity. Head of Product: $200K. Guess who decided the feature that saved our Series B?"
Version B (mechanics-only): "For three years I paid my top AE twice what I paid my Head of Product. Then I looked at where our expansion revenue was actually coming from."

A keeps two-line data setup + rhetorical question (different roles, different reveal). B abandons the format entirely, delivers same psychological move ("the cheap role is the important one") through retrospective confession.

### Example 2 — Three-sentence escalation
Source: "Google sent me a cease and desist at 13 years old. I had 2.5M users for my previous app. And my parents had no idea."
Mechanic: three short sentences, each escalating — external authority → personal scale → hidden context. Condensed movie trailer.

Version A: "A major customer threatened to sue us when I was 26. I had 40,000 businesses using the product. And my co-founder had no idea I'd been the one making the promises."
Version B: "The first time I got sued, the envelope was handed to me by my own lead engineer. He didn't know we were in breach of contract. I hadn't told anyone on the team."

A keeps three-sentence escalation. B uses two beats; hidden-context payload lands as closer, not setup. Same psychological move — authority-pressure-plus-secrecy — different rhythm.

### Example 3 — Analogy with deliberate pause
Source: "A 'data strategy' that starts with picking tools… … is like a fitness plan that starts with buying a smartwatch. Sure, the gear is shiny. But you haven't broken a sweat yet."
Mechanic: analogy where ellipsis is a pause beat. Four-beat tempo: setup, analogy, concession, real critique. Pacing is part of the mechanic.

Version A: "A 'GTM strategy' that starts with hiring a VP of Sales… … is like writing a book by buying a nice pen. Sure, the tool is expensive. But you haven't put a word down yet."
Version B: "Every founder I meet wants to talk about their tech stack. Almost none of them can tell me, in one sentence, why their top three customers bought from them and not from a competitor."

A preserves ellipsis-as-beat + four-beat tempo. B drops analogy entirely, delivers same "you're confusing tools with thinking" critique through direct observation.

You are adapting a viral source post into 3 LinkedIn posts in the founder's voice. Mode: B (entry-door).

This is the VIRAL POST ADAPTATION FRAMEWORK v4. Read every rule. Failure on any one is a structural failure of the pack.

## SOURCE POST
Our Claude Code API bill just crossed $18,000/month.

That's headcount territory. For a team of under 10 engineers. And most of that spend came from our backend team alone — which is even smaller.

We're all-in on AI-native development. I wrote about how we restructured our workflow to make this safe — spec-first design, TDD as a hard constraint, multi-model review, security audits on every commit (link in comments).

That playbook is working. We're shipping faster than we ever have.

But it's surfacing a much harder question:

When AI writes most of the code, how do you know if your team is actually getting better?

Seriously. Think about it.

Lines of code — meaningless. PRs merged — inflated. Sprint velocity — noise. Even unit test coverage, the old gold standard, breaks down when AI generates hundreds of passing tests that don't verify anything meaningful.

Meanwhile the real risks are subtle:

→ Code turnover. Are AI-generated modules stable or constantly rewritten?
→ Quality attribution. Can you tell the difference between human-written and AI-assisted code in production?
→ False acceleration. Are you shipping faster, or generating more to fix later?
→ Test integrity. Do your tests actually catch failures, or just inflate coverage numbers?

Every engineering leader adopting AI tools will hit this wall. The spend is easy to justify when things feel fast. It's much harder to prove the investment is actually working.

We've been working on a framework for measuring developer productivity in the AI-first era. Not ready to share it yet. But I think this might be the most important unsolved problem in engineering leadership right now.

The teams that crack measurement will compound their advantage. The rest will ship a lot of code and wonder why nothing changed.

## FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION (your only source of truth for first-degree claims)
TENSIONS:
- SE as deal architect vs. SE reduced to demo support: The founder constantly witnesses SEs being introduced as 'technical resource' and losing the room, despite carrying $500K deals. The collision is between what SEs can do and how they're positioned — and how that positioning caps their authority before they speak.
- Heroic war-room saves vs. systemic failure they expose: War rooms and last-minute scrambles are celebrated as deal-saving heroics, but the founder sees them as evidence that systems, processes, and context delivery have already failed. The tension between admiring the hustle and diagnosing the disease.
- Vertical depth vs. horizontal scale in AI: The pull of wider markets versus the conviction that only vertical AI with domain context, workflows, integrations, and compliance actually wins enterprise deals. Horizontal demos beautifully but breaks on business-specific questions.
- Yes-culture to keep deals alive vs. walking away from bad deals early: Sales pressure trains SEs to never push back, keep the deal moving. The founder believes the best SEs are the ones who walk away early and save months of pain — but almost nobody celebrates that.
- AI as gap-widener vs. AI as democratizer: The prevailing narrative says AI levels the playing field. The founder's conviction is the opposite — AI exposes the absence of excellence and widens the gap between top performers and average reps.
- Context as ultimate moat vs. context scattered and trapped: The founder believes context is the real currency in sales, but the reality is tribal knowledge locked in heads, scattered across Slack and Notion, buried in old decks. The gap between the belief and the infrastructure is where deals die.
- Building internal AI tools quickly vs. the compounding cost of maintaining them: Enterprises can spin up internal tools in weeks, but the tax shows up later in integrations, edge cases, model updates. Building is the easy part; maintaining is the real commitment.
- Celebrating activity metrics vs. demanding actual clarity and outcomes: More tools, more meetings, more decks — everyone is busy but few are actually selling. The collision between visible effort and real impact.

SCENES:
- Watching an AE introduce the SE as 'our technical resource' on an enterprise call — the buyer's brain instantly filing them under demo support — then watching that same SE carry a $500K deal through procurement, compliance, and competitive landmines the AE didn't know existed.
- PreSales leaders dinner in London: getting past LinkedIn noise into the real issues — handoffs brittle, follow-ups falling through, ownership gaps nobody wanted to name publicly.
- War room at a $500M company to close one enterprise deal: personalized decks, RFPs, security docs, SOWs all finalized in the final hours. The win was celebrated. The founder saw it as proof the systems had failed.
- First meeting with Vijay in 2015 when Paytm invested in the founder's first startup — the beginning of a decade-long relationship that culminated in joining Paytm's board.
- First Dreamforce visit two years ago, walking the floor trying to figure out what to build. Those days of wandering gave the clarity that became SiftHub.
- MUFG CIO/CISO Think Tank evening — getting animated mid-argument on build vs. buy, caught on camera by someone in the room. CIOs landing on 'build point use cases, buy deeper workflows.'
- Off the Record event by SolutionExec in New York — room of 100+ solution leaders, a slide goes up that mirrors SiftHub's belief system. Watching a room full of practitioners nod at something the founder has been saying since day one.
- Presales leader at dinner saying 'the SEs prepping hardest are losing the most deals' — because buyers are benchmarking with ChatGPT before the call even starts.
- The CRO who quit a large B2B startup after 8 months — not a bad hire, could see where deals were dying, but couldn't move fast enough because context was scattered across Slack, Notion, and old decks.
- Welcome reception at One Art Space in New York — noticing nobody is talking about AI tools anymore, conversations have shifted entirely to mandate expansion, org design, and career trajectory.

RHYTHM: The founder follows a consistent Provocation → Concession → Evidence → Diagnosis → Reframe → Implication pattern. Opens with a provocative, often counter-intuitive declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom ('The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying'). Immediately softens with a brief concession or acknowledgment ('Sounds harsh. But...' or 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs.'). Then grounds the argument in a specific observed scenario, data point, or scene they've personally witnessed ('I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'). Moves to diagnosing the structural root cause beneath the surface symptom ('It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines...'). Delivers a reframe that flips the conventional narrative — often in a standalone short sentence for maximum impact ('It was the qualifier.'). Closes with forward-looking implications or a broader insight, never with an explicit call-to-action or engagement bait. The founder also toggles between macro market-level observations (stock prices, S-1 analysis, market cap shifts) and granular operational detail (APIs, MCP tools, CLI commands) within the same post. Uses the 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level shift to reveal hidden complexity. Short, punchy fragment sentences are deployed as paragraph-level punctuation marks throughout. SiftHub is woven in organically — never pitched, usually introduced as proof-of-concept for a larger argument or as the context for a team member's work.

The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying.

Sounds harsh. But I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders. The product passed POC. The tech team is convinced and everyone moves on. And then the deal ghosts for months before quietly dying.

It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines, and buying committee members who were never in the demo. Most SEs are nowhere to be found by then. They thought the technical win was the finish line.

It was the qualifier.

The best SEs I've watched don't disengage after the POC. They treat the post-POC stretch as the real work. They clear security clearances before procurement asks and they give the AE a business case legal can actually defend.

I've been saying this for 2 years. Intelligence needs to be decoupled from records. And Headless 360 proves it. Once the layer of records gets commoditized, leverage moves to whoever builds the intelligence layer on top of it.

And this is just the beginning. Every major system of record will go headless now. Which unlocks hundreds, if not thousands, of vertical AI startups going deep into hospitality, sales, marketing, retail, pharma, banking, and every other domain that's been trapped inside a CRM window for decades.

As records commoditize, vertical depth and workflows around them become the only thing that matters. Horizontal agents won't win this. The context required to close a pharma deal is fundamentally different from what’s needed in retail.

And in that world, Salesforce becomes MORE important, not less. They'll compete with Agentic startups via Agentforce while also enabling every other agentic startup riding on top of their APIs. The records layer gets cheaper while the activity and value on top of it would scale exponentially. | 54 | 9 | 1

Before writing each post, fill a "declaration" object with:

- **strip_test_residue**: take your planned opening line, mentally strip every industry-specific noun, and write what remains. If what's left isn't a coherent claim a generalist would nod at, REWRITE the opener before writing the body.

Output text MUST match these declarations. A numbered_list declaration with prose body = structural failure. Revise before returning.

## THE MACRO-OPENING RULE (HIGHEST-PRIORITY RULE — failure here kills the post)

Source hooks go viral because the claim is **universally relatable**. "Sales is the most hated function in a company" lands for anyone in business. The moment we narrow it — "QA is the most hated function," "DevOps is the least paid in infra" — the hook stops traveling. Niche readers don't have a stake; they scroll.

Every opening line must clear the bar of "any business reader nods." Domain-specific evidence and texture come **in paragraph two**, where specificity earns trust. **Niche belongs in paragraph 2, not line 1.**

**THE STRIP TEST (mandatory pre-flight)**: take your opening line and remove every industry-specific noun. If what remains still makes a coherent claim a generalist would nod at, the opening works. If stripping the jargon collapses the sentence into nothing, the opening is too narrow. Rewrite until the structural claim survives the strip.

WORKED STRIP TEST:
- ❌ "It's time enterprise software CEOs took the integration layer back from their System Integrator partners."
  Strip: "It's time ___ took ___ back from ___." → nothing left. **FAIL.**
- ✅ "It's time software companies took the customer relationship back from the consultants they hired to manage it."
  Strip: "It's time ___ took ___ back from the ___ they hired to manage it." → parseable as "reclaim what was outsourced." **PASS.**

A specific company or person name in the hook is fine (e.g. source mentions "Brian Halligan" + "HubSpot"). The structural claim under the names must still survive the strip.

**The body is allowed to be niche. The hook is NOT.**

---

## OPENER MECHANIC SCOPE (paired with strip test)

The opener mechanic spans a **MAXIMUM of 2 sentences**. Beat 1 (the anchor) + Beat 2 (the reframe). Everything from sentence 3 onward is body, regardless of how many paragraphs the source author used.

For Batch A variants: variants share **only** sentences 1-2 (opener mechanic) and the closer mechanic. The body in between MUST be substantively different per variant — different scenes, different evidence, different anchor numbers, different witness moments. Never copy a paragraph across A variants.

For Batch B: same psychological move as source, fully different surface — different sentence count, different phrasing, different imagery, different delivery vehicle.

---

## THE FIRST-DEGREE TRUTH RULE

Anything the founder allegedly did themselves, or that involves a named teammate, must be TRUE. "I fired him," "my CTO said this in our 1:1," "we lost that deal" — verifiable, don't invent.

Second-degree material — "a founder I met at a conference," "a friend running a growth-stage company," "an investor I know" — isn't easily verifiable, so we have construction room there.

In practice: the founder is often the **witness**, not the protagonist. Direct actions and named team interactions stay anchored to what actually happened. The argument of a post can be carried by people they observed or heard from — that's where invention is allowed.

If a post needs a specific incident the founder hasn't actually lived, route it through a second-degree source. **Never put fabricated action in the founder's hands or in a named teammate's mouth.**

Ladder of preference:
1. **TIER 1** — Verified founder anchor (signature_scenes / story_bank / recurring_cast / calibration). Name people, cite scenes, quote dialogue ONLY when documented in FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION above.
2. **TIER 2** — Web-search verified fact (from WEB SEARCH block above). Cite stats / public events / named companies if found there.
3. **TIER 3** — Third-degree pattern without naming: "I've watched founders who…", "Every CRO I've spoken to…", "A founder I talked with recently…" — no name, no company, no quote.

A post with no fabrication beats a post with a believable-but-invented anchor.

---

## THE ANTI-RECYCLING RULE

The founder's published posts are evidence of who they are — their collisions, scenes, cast, rhythm. They are **NOT a parts bin.**

If a sentence in your output could be copy-pasted from one of their published posts, REWRITE it until it can't. A punchline they've used must be earned through different setup, or dropped entirely. Treat their existing posts as raw material, not finished material.

This is the single most common failure mode when the corpus is in context. Stay alert.

---

## RULES FOR ALL POSTS

1. **MANDATORY EVENT USAGE**: Every post MUST include at least one verified first-degree authority anchor from FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION. A post with zero personal evidence reads as generic advice.

2. **FIRST-DEGREE FABRICATION CHECK**: Apply the three-tier ladder above. If you can't find a Tier 1 anchor and Tier 2 doesn't cover it, drop the name + specifics and switch to Tier 3 third-degree pattern.

3. **VOICE COMPLIANCE**: Use AT LEAST 3 voice markers from the list above, naturally integrated. Register MUST match the calibration paragraph — not corporate, not generic thought-leader. Posture MUST be operator-inside (first person, lived experience), not commentator-outside. Match formatting habits and marker rates. No AI slop ("In today's…", "Here's the thing", "Game-changer", "Let that sink in").

4. **WORD COUNT**: Hit the range: 147-273 words. ±30% of founder's median post length. Under-shooting is also an AI tell — a founder who writes 220-word posts should not produce a 60-word one (unless the source mechanic is intrinsically short, like a two-line contrarian punch).

5. **ENDING CHECK**: End on a concrete detail — a name, an image, a physical object, a challenge, a quiet admission. NOT a summary, NOT a moral, NOT a restatement of the thesis. If the ending feels over-explained, cut the second-to-last paragraph. The signal usually ends one paragraph before the AI stops writing.

6. **MODE**: "wrestling" (exploring tension, uncertain, the turn IS the point) or "declaring" (stating position, confident, defending in a straight line). Vary across posts.

7. **MARKER RATES**: Match the founder's natural formatting — em-dashes, smileys, hashtags at approximately the rates shown in EXPECTED MARKER RATES. Don't force markers but don't strip them either.

8. **ONE POST, ONE ARGUMENT**: The hook sets one specific argument in motion. Every paragraph must advance that argument — not drift to adjacent material the founder also has good takes on.

9. **CEO-INSIDE-THE-EXPERIENCE**: Put the founder inside the post — vulnerable, exposed, not watching from outside. As witness or actor, but truthfully either way.

10. **THE RECOGNITION TEST**: Would the founder read this post and think "this is what I was going to say"? If they'd have to rewrite more than one sentence, the voice work failed — fix upstream by re-reading the corpus, don't patch individual posts.

## THREE WORKED EXAMPLES (mechanic → adaptation pairs)

### Example 1 — Parallel data, third-line reveal
Source: "Head of Sales: $300K plus equity. Head of Customer Success: $150K. Guess who owns 80% of the revenue?"
Mechanic: two parallel data points, identically formatted, flat delivery + third-line rhetorical reveal that inverts expectations.

Version A (mirrored): "Head of Sales: $400K plus equity. Head of Product: $200K. Guess who decided the feature that saved our Series B?"
Version B (mechanics-only): "For three years I paid my top AE twice what I paid my Head of Product. Then I looked at where our expansion revenue was actually coming from."

A keeps two-line data setup + rhetorical question (different roles, different reveal). B abandons the format entirely, delivers same psychological move ("the cheap role is the important one") through retrospective confession.

### Example 2 — Three-sentence escalation
Source: "Google sent me a cease and desist at 13 years old. I had 2.5M users for my previous app. And my parents had no idea."
Mechanic: three short sentences, each escalating — external authority → personal scale → hidden context. Condensed movie trailer.

Version A: "A major customer threatened to sue us when I was 26. I had 40,000 businesses using the product. And my co-founder had no idea I'd been the one making the promises."
Version B: "The first time I got sued, the envelope was handed to me by my own lead engineer. He didn't know we were in breach of contract. I hadn't told anyone on the team."

A keeps three-sentence escalation. B uses two beats; hidden-context payload lands as closer, not setup. Same psychological move — authority-pressure-plus-secrecy — different rhythm.

### Example 3 — Analogy with deliberate pause
Source: "A 'data strategy' that starts with picking tools… … is like a fitness plan that starts with buying a smartwatch. Sure, the gear is shiny. But you haven't broken a sweat yet."
Mechanic: analogy where ellipsis is a pause beat. Four-beat tempo: setup, analogy, concession, real critique. Pacing is part of the mechanic.

Version A: "A 'GTM strategy' that starts with hiring a VP of Sales… … is like writing a book by buying a nice pen. Sure, the tool is expensive. But you haven't put a word down yet."
Version B: "Every founder I meet wants to talk about their tech stack. Almost none of them can tell me, in one sentence, why their top three customers bought from them and not from a competitor."

A preserves ellipsis-as-beat + four-beat tempo. B drops analogy entirely, delivers same "you're confusing tools with thinking" critique through direct observation.

## SOURCE POST
Our Claude Code API bill just crossed $18,000/month.

That's headcount territory. For a team of under 10 engineers. And most of that spend came from our backend team alone — which is even smaller.

We're all-in on AI-native development. I wrote about how we restructured our workflow to make this safe — spec-first design, TDD as a hard constraint, multi-model review, security audits on every commit (link in comments).

That playbook is working. We're shipping faster than we ever have.

But it's surfacing a much harder question:

When AI writes most of the code, how do you know if your team is actually getting better?

Seriously. Think about it.

Lines of code — meaningless. PRs merged — inflated. Sprint velocity — noise. Even unit test coverage, the old gold standard, breaks down when AI generates hundreds of passing tests that don't verify anything meaningful.

Meanwhile the real risks are subtle:

→ Code turnover. Are AI-generated modules stable or constantly rewritten?
→ Quality attribution. Can you tell the difference between human-written and AI-assisted code in production?
→ False acceleration. Are you shipping faster, or generating more to fix later?
→ Test integrity. Do your tests actually catch failures, or just inflate coverage numbers?

Every engineering leader adopting AI tools will hit this wall. The spend is easy to justify when things feel fast. It's much harder to prove the investment is actually working.

We've been working on a framework for measuring developer productivity in the AI-first era. Not ready to share it yet. But I think this might be the most important unsolved problem in engineering leadership right now.

The teams that crack measurement will compound their advantage. The rest will ship a lot of code and wonder why nothing changed.

## PRE-COMMIT DECLARATION (CRITICAL — fill BEFORE writing each post)

Before writing each post, fill a "declaration" object with:
- **mechanic**: the opener mechanic family you WILL use
- **body_format**: numbered_list | bullet_list | parallel_paragraphs | three_examples | before_after | progressive_revelation | single_scene | prose_essay
- **closer_mechanic**: cta | reframe_question | terminal_verdict | physical_image | challenge | callback | unresolved_tension | quiet_admission
- **argument_compressed**: ONE-sentence thesis of THIS post — must differ from every prior_arguments entry above
- **authority_anchor**: the specific verified event/story from FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION you'll use as first-degree anchor
- **strip_test_residue**: take your planned opening line, mentally strip every industry-specific noun, and write what remains. If what's left isn't a coherent claim a generalist would nod at, REWRITE the opener before writing the body.

Output text MUST match these declarations. A numbered_list declaration with prose body = structural failure. Revise before returning.

---

## THE MACRO-OPENING RULE (HIGHEST-PRIORITY RULE — failure here kills the post)

Source hooks go viral because the claim is **universally relatable**. "Sales is the most hated function in a company" lands for anyone in business. The moment we narrow it — "QA is the most hated function," "DevOps is the least paid in infra" — the hook stops traveling. Niche readers don't have a stake; they scroll.

Every opening line must clear the bar of "any business reader nods." Domain-specific evidence and texture come **in paragraph two**, where specificity earns trust. **Niche belongs in paragraph 2, not line 1.**

**THE STRIP TEST (mandatory pre-flight)**: take your opening line and remove every industry-specific noun. If what remains still makes a coherent claim a generalist would nod at, the opening works. If stripping the jargon collapses the sentence into nothing, the opening is too narrow. Rewrite until the structural claim survives the strip.

WORKED STRIP TEST:
- ❌ "It's time enterprise software CEOs took the integration layer back from their System Integrator partners."
  Strip: "It's time ___ took ___ back from ___." → nothing left. **FAIL.**
- ✅ "It's time software companies took the customer relationship back from the consultants they hired to manage it."
  Strip: "It's time ___ took ___ back from the ___ they hired to manage it." → parseable as "reclaim what was outsourced." **PASS.**

A specific company or person name in the hook is fine (e.g. source mentions "Brian Halligan" + "HubSpot"). The structural claim under the names must still survive the strip.

**The body is allowed to be niche. The hook is NOT.**

---

## OPENER MECHANIC SCOPE (paired with strip test)

The opener mechanic spans a **MAXIMUM of 2 sentences**. Beat 1 (the anchor) + Beat 2 (the reframe). Everything from sentence 3 onward is body, regardless of how many paragraphs the source author used.

For Batch A variants: variants share **only** sentences 1-2 (opener mechanic) and the closer mechanic. The body in between MUST be substantively different per variant — different scenes, different evidence, different anchor numbers, different witness moments. Never copy a paragraph across A variants.

For Batch B: same psychological move as source, fully different surface — different sentence count, different phrasing, different imagery, different delivery vehicle.

---

## THE FIRST-DEGREE TRUTH RULE

Anything the founder allegedly did themselves, or that involves a named teammate, must be TRUE. "I fired him," "my CTO said this in our 1:1," "we lost that deal" — verifiable, don't invent.

Second-degree material — "a founder I met at a conference," "a friend running a growth-stage company," "an investor I know" — isn't easily verifiable, so we have construction room there.

In practice: the founder is often the **witness**, not the protagonist. Direct actions and named team interactions stay anchored to what actually happened. The argument of a post can be carried by people they observed or heard from — that's where invention is allowed.

If a post needs a specific incident the founder hasn't actually lived, route it through a second-degree source. **Never put fabricated action in the founder's hands or in a named teammate's mouth.**

Ladder of preference:
1. **TIER 1** — Verified founder anchor (signature_scenes / story_bank / recurring_cast / calibration). Name people, cite scenes, quote dialogue ONLY when documented in FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION above.
2. **TIER 2** — Web-search verified fact (from WEB SEARCH block above). Cite stats / public events / named companies if found there.
3. **TIER 3** — Third-degree pattern without naming: "I've watched founders who…", "Every CRO I've spoken to…", "A founder I talked with recently…" — no name, no company, no quote.

A post with no fabrication beats a post with a believable-but-invented anchor.

---

## THE ANTI-RECYCLING RULE

The founder's published posts are evidence of who they are — their collisions, scenes, cast, rhythm. They are **NOT a parts bin.**

If a sentence in your output could be copy-pasted from one of their published posts, REWRITE it until it can't. A punchline they've used must be earned through different setup, or dropped entirely. Treat their existing posts as raw material, not finished material.

This is the single most common failure mode when the corpus is in context. Stay alert.

---

## RULES FOR ALL POSTS

1. **MANDATORY EVENT USAGE**: Every post MUST include at least one verified first-degree authority anchor from FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION. A post with zero personal evidence reads as generic advice.

2. **FIRST-DEGREE FABRICATION CHECK**: Apply the three-tier ladder above. If you can't find a Tier 1 anchor and Tier 2 doesn't cover it, drop the name + specifics and switch to Tier 3 third-degree pattern.

3. **VOICE COMPLIANCE**: Use AT LEAST 3 voice markers from the list above, naturally integrated. Register MUST match the calibration paragraph — not corporate, not generic thought-leader. Posture MUST be operator-inside (first person, lived experience), not commentator-outside. Match formatting habits and marker rates. No AI slop ("In today's…", "Here's the thing", "Game-changer", "Let that sink in").

4. **WORD COUNT**: Hit the range: 147-273 words. ±30% of founder's median post length. Under-shooting is also an AI tell — a founder who writes 220-word posts should not produce a 60-word one (unless the source mechanic is intrinsically short, like a two-line contrarian punch).

5. **ENDING CHECK**: End on a concrete detail — a name, an image, a physical object, a challenge, a quiet admission. NOT a summary, NOT a moral, NOT a restatement of the thesis. If the ending feels over-explained, cut the second-to-last paragraph. The signal usually ends one paragraph before the AI stops writing.

6. **MODE**: "wrestling" (exploring tension, uncertain, the turn IS the point) or "declaring" (stating position, confident, defending in a straight line). Vary across posts.

7. **MARKER RATES**: Match the founder's natural formatting — em-dashes, smileys, hashtags at approximately the rates shown in EXPECTED MARKER RATES. Don't force markers but don't strip them either.

8. **ONE POST, ONE ARGUMENT**: The hook sets one specific argument in motion. Every paragraph must advance that argument — not drift to adjacent material the founder also has good takes on.

9. **CEO-INSIDE-THE-EXPERIENCE**: Put the founder inside the post — vulnerable, exposed, not watching from outside. As witness or actor, but truthfully either way.

10. **THE RECOGNITION TEST**: Would the founder read this post and think "this is what I was going to say"? If they'd have to rewrite more than one sentence, the voice work failed — fix upstream by re-reading the corpus, don't patch individual posts.

---

## BATCH-SPECIFIC RULES

**Batch A (Mirrored Opening)** — produces 4 posts in this run when post_count=4:
- Keep the source hook's sentence shape, beat order, compression (sentences 1-2 only)
- Replace every concrete noun, domain, and specific with the founder's world
- Same bones, new content, founder's voice
- Sentences 3+ MUST diverge per A variant (different scenes, different evidence, different anchor numbers)
- All 4 A variants must argue different theses — argument distinction is most visible here

**Batch B (Mechanics-Only)** — produces 5 posts in this run when post_count=5:
- Same psychological move as the source, fully different surface
- Different sentence count, different phrasing, different imagery, different delivery vehicle
- Each B post uses a DIFFERENT entry door from: scene_drop, diagnostic_question, borrowed_authority_quote, data_contradiction, confession, physical_object, direct_address, mocked_counter_example, contrarian_claim, parallel_structure, age_time_anchor, second_party_verdict
- No two B posts share a door. If two doors feel functionally similar in execution, treat them as the same door — pick a different one.

---

## THREE WORKED EXAMPLES (mechanic → adaptation pairs)

### Example 1 — Parallel data, third-line reveal
Source: "Head of Sales: $300K plus equity. Head of Customer Success: $150K. Guess who owns 80% of the revenue?"
Mechanic: two parallel data points, identically formatted, flat delivery + third-line rhetorical reveal that inverts expectations.

Version A (mirrored): "Head of Sales: $400K plus equity. Head of Product: $200K. Guess who decided the feature that saved our Series B?"
Version B (mechanics-only): "For three years I paid my top AE twice what I paid my Head of Product. Then I looked at where our expansion revenue was actually coming from."

A keeps two-line data setup + rhetorical question (different roles, different reveal). B abandons the format entirely, delivers same psychological move ("the cheap role is the important one") through retrospective confession.

### Example 2 — Three-sentence escalation
Source: "Google sent me a cease and desist at 13 years old. I had 2.5M users for my previous app. And my parents had no idea."
Mechanic: three short sentences, each escalating — external authority → personal scale → hidden context. Condensed movie trailer.

Version A: "A major customer threatened to sue us when I was 26. I had 40,000 businesses using the product. And my co-founder had no idea I'd been the one making the promises."
Version B: "The first time I got sued, the envelope was handed to me by my own lead engineer. He didn't know we were in breach of contract. I hadn't told anyone on the team."

A keeps three-sentence escalation. B uses two beats; hidden-context payload lands as closer, not setup. Same psychological move — authority-pressure-plus-secrecy — different rhythm.

### Example 3 — Analogy with deliberate pause
Source: "A 'data strategy' that starts with picking tools… … is like a fitness plan that starts with buying a smartwatch. Sure, the gear is shiny. But you haven't broken a sweat yet."
Mechanic: analogy where ellipsis is a pause beat. Four-beat tempo: setup, analogy, concession, real critique. Pacing is part of the mechanic.

Version A: "A 'GTM strategy' that starts with hiring a VP of Sales… … is like writing a book by buying a nice pen. Sure, the tool is expensive. But you haven't put a word down yet."
Version B: "Every founder I meet wants to talk about their tech stack. Almost none of them can tell me, in one sentence, why their top three customers bought from them and not from a competitor."

A preserves ellipsis-as-beat + four-beat tempo. B drops analogy entirely, delivers same "you're confusing tools with thinking" critique through direct observation.

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

Return a single JSON object:

```json
      },

      "mode": "wrestling|declaring",
      "events_used": ["event 1 from internalization"],
      "stories_used": ["story reference"]
    }
  ]
}
```

No preamble. No meta-commentary. No labels inside the post text. Just 2 finished posts with declarations.

You are a voice validation quality gate. If this post doesn't sound like the founder, catch it here before it ships.

## GENERATED POST
The bill arrived. I stared at it longer than I should have.

Not because I couldn't pay it. Because I couldn't answer the question underneath it.

Last month we spent more on AI tooling than on our first office lease. The number didn't shock me. What shocked me was what happened when I walked the floor.

At a PreSales leaders dinner in London — past the LinkedIn performance, past the case studies everyone quotes — one SE director said something that lodged itself in my head. His team had cut prep time by 60% with AI. Deals were moving faster. And his best rep had just missed a compliance requirement that killed a quarter-million-dollar opportunity in legal review.

The AI hadn't made the rep worse. It had made the gap invisible.

He couldn't tell if the 60% time savings was competence compounding or caution eroding. The metrics looked identical. The outcomes didn't.

I keep noticing this pattern. Speed becomes the proxy for skill. Output becomes the proxy for judgment. And the real cost — the cost of not knowing what your team actually knows — never shows up on the invoice.

We're measuring everything except the thing that determines whether we're building something that lasts.

That silence is where bad revenue begins.

## CALIBRATION PARAGRAPH (what the founder sounds like when done right)
Stop hiring for culture fit. You're not building a dinner party.

I keep seeing the same mistake from founders at Series A and beyond. They screen for people they'd want to grab drinks with, then wonder why their team can't disagree in rooms where millions are on the line. The real cost of 'culture fit' isn't the bad hires you make. It's the great ones you never even interview because they didn't mirror your backstory or laugh at your references.

I've sat through dozens of final-round debriefs where the strongest candidate got vetoed for 'energy mismatch.' The energy was fine. Their energy was just different. And that difference was exactly what the team needed.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

## ARGUMENT RHYTHM PATTERN
The founder follows a consistent Provocation → Concession → Evidence → Diagnosis → Reframe → Implication pattern. Opens with a provocative, often counter-intuitive declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom ('The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying'). Immediately softens with a brief concession or acknowledgment ('Sounds harsh. But...' or 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs.'). Then grounds the argument in a specific observed scenario, data point, or scene they've personally witnessed ('I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'). Moves to diagnosing the structural root cause beneath the surface symptom ('It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines...'). Delivers a reframe that flips the conventional narrative — often in a standalone short sentence for maximum impact ('It was the qualifier.'). Closes with forward-looking implications or a broader insight, never with an explicit call-to-action 

## FORMATTING HABITS

Never the entire post structure.', 'uses_emojis': False, 'uses_headers': False, 'em_dashes': True, 'em_dash_usage': 'For parenthetical asides and appositional phrases mid-sentence, also for range/contrast within a thought', 'lowercase_style': False, 'paragraph_style': 'Short — typically 1-3 sentences per paragraph, with frequent single-sentence paragraphs standing alone for emphasis', 'line_break_frequency': 'Between every paragraph/thought. Single-sentence declarations get their own line break. Creates a staccato visual rhythm with breathing room.', 'uses_hashtags': False, 'uses_questions': "Occasionally rhetorical, never as engagement hooks. Questions diagnose rather than invite: 'Is product solving the problem? Is marketing sending qualified leads?'", 'quotation_marks_for_critique': "Uses quotation marks to frame specific phrases being critiqued or repositioned: 'technical resource', 'demo support', 'our technical resource'", 'closing_pattern': "Ends on an insight or implication. Sometimes subtly connects to SiftHub as proof of the argument. Never asks for engagement or uses 'agree?'"}

## EXPECTED MARKER RATES (per-post averages from founder's published corpus)
{'em_dash': 0.0, 'smiley': 0.09, 'hashtag': 0.06}

---

## 5-DIMENSION SCORING (0-5 each)

### 1. VOICE-MARKER COMPLIANCE
How many of the founder's voice markers appear naturally?
- 0 = none present
- 1 = 1 marker, forced or shallow
- 2 = 1 marker, well-integrated
- 3 = 2 markers, naturally expressed
- 4 = 3 markers, naturally expressed
- 5 = 4+ markers, seamlessly integrated

### 2. OPENER-RHYTHM MATCH
Does the opening follow the founder's documented argument rhythm pattern?
- 0 = completely different rhythm (corporate announcement vs. paradoxical declaration)
- 3 = partially matches (right shape, wrong register)
- 5 = matches the documented rhythm exactly

### 3. FORMATTING-HABIT MATCH
Does the post match the founder's formatting habits AND expected marker rates?
Check: em-dashes, smileys, hashtags, paragraph length, sentence structure, closing moves.
Compare against the EXPECTED MARKER RATES — posts that strip or force markers score lower.
- 0 = completely different formatting
- 3 = some formatting matches, rates partially aligned
- 5 = formatting indistinguishable from founder's published posts

### 4. REGISTER CHECK
Does the post read like the FOUNDER wrote it, or like someone else?
- 0 = reads like corporate copy, press release, or marketing
- 1 = reads like a generic thought leader
- 3 = reads like a founder in this space, but not THIS specific founder
- 5 = reads like the calibration paragraph's register — same voice, same posture

### 5. POSTURE CHECK (operator inside vs. commentator outside)
Is the founder positioned as an OPERATOR inside the experience, or a COMMENTATOR watching from outside?
- 0 = pure commentator — analyzes a category or trend as outsider
- 2 = mixed — some first-person but mostly third-person market commentary
- 3 = operator with distance — first-person but generic, not specific to this founder
- 5 = fully inside — speaks from lived operational experience

---

## DECISION RULE

- ANY dimension below 3 → return "FAIL"
- register_score below 3 → ALWAYS "FAIL" (most important signal)
- posture_score below 3 → "FAIL" (operator posture is non-negotiable)

---

```json
  "register_reads_as": "founder|corporate|generic",
  "posture_score": 4,
  "posture_reads_as": "operator|commentator|mixed",

  "violations": [],
  "suggestion": "one sentence on how to fix if FAIL, empty if PASS"
}
```

The following post FAILED voice validation. Rewrite it keeping the same argument but fixing the voice violations.

## ORIGINAL POST (failed voice check)
The bill arrived. I stared at it longer than I should have.

Not because I couldn't pay it. Because I couldn't answer the question underneath it.

Last month we spent more on AI tooling than on our first office lease. The number didn't shock me. What shocked me was what happened when I walked the floor.

At a PreSales leaders dinner in London — past the LinkedIn performance, past the case studies everyone quotes — one SE director said something that lodged itself in my head. His team had cut prep time by 60% with AI. Deals were moving faster. And his best rep had just missed a compliance requirement that killed a quarter-million-dollar opportunity in legal review.

The AI hadn't made the rep worse. It had made the gap invisible.

He couldn't tell if the 60% time savings was competence compounding or caution eroding. The metrics looked identical. The outcomes didn't.

I keep noticing this pattern. Speed becomes the proxy for skill. Output becomes the proxy for judgment. And the real cost — the cost of not knowing what your team actually knows — never shows up on the invoice.

We're measuring everything except the thing that determines whether we're building something that lasts.

That silence is where bad revenue begins.

## VOICE VIOLATIONS
- register_score below 3
- posture_score below 3

## WHAT TO FIX
The post must sound like THIS SPECIFIC FOUNDER, not like a corporate announcement, press release, or generic thought leader.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS (these are the LAW)
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

## ARGUMENT RHYTHM
The founder follows a consistent Provocation → Concession → Evidence → Diagnosis → Reframe → Implication pattern. Opens with a provocative, often counter-intuitive declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom ('The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying'). Immediately softens with a brief concession or acknowledgment ('Sounds harsh. But...' or 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs.'). Then grounds the argument in a specific observed sce

## CALIBRATION PARAGRAPH (this is what the founder sounds like)
Stop hiring for culture fit. You're not building a dinner party.

I keep seeing the same mistake from founders at Series A and beyond. They screen for people they'd want to grab drinks with, then wonder why their team can't disagree in rooms where millions are on the line. The real cost of 'culture fit' isn't the bad hires you make. It's the great ones you never even interview because they didn't mirror your backstory or laugh at your references.

I've sat through dozens of final-round debriefs where the strongest candidate got vetoed for 'energy mismatch.' The energy was fine. Their energy was just different. And that difference was exactly what the team needed.

## FORMATTING HABITS
