traces.prompt:
You are a voice validation quality gate. If this post doesn't sound like the founder, catch it here before it ships.

## GENERATED POST
More tools. More headcount. More process documentation.

Same result every quarter.

I keep hearing the same story from presales leaders—past the LinkedIn performance, past the posts, into what actually breaks. Handoffs brittle. Follow-ups falling through. Ownership gaps nobody wanted to name publicly in THEIR own companies. The room knew the pattern. Nobody had fixed it.

The assumption is always the same: the next hire will fix it. Better toolkit, stronger methodology, more senior title.

Nobody asks why the role keeps failing.

Not because the SE wasn't talented. Because the structure around the SE was the real problem.

You can hire the most technically gifted solutions engineer in the world and position them as 'our technical resource.' You give them no real authority, involve them once the direction is already locked, then wonder why they can't save deals in the final hour.

The org calls it a talent problem and hires again.

But here's what the industry is less comfortable saying: SEs let this happen too. We trained ourselves to execute inside systems, not shape them. To improve the brief, not question whether the brief was right.

The SEs who do have real influence got there by refusing to accept the seat they were given—and going to find a better one themselves.

Two failures. Same outcome.

The real cost isn't the bad hire. It's the great one who learns to perform inside a broken frame until they quit—or worse, until they stop trying to change it.

## CALIBRATION PARAGRAPH (what the founder sounds like when done right)
Stop hiring for culture fit. You're not building a dinner party.

I keep seeing the same mistake from founders at Series A and beyond. They screen for people they'd want to grab drinks with, then wonder why their team can't disagree in rooms where millions are on the line. The real cost of 'culture fit' isn't the bad hires you make. It's the great ones you never even interview because they didn't mirror your backstory or laugh at your references.

I've sat through dozens of final-round debriefs where the strongest candidate got vetoed for 'energy mismatch.' The energy was fine. Their energy was just different. And that difference was exactly what the team needed.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

## ARGUMENT RHYTHM PATTERN
The founder follows a consistent Provocation → Concession → Evidence → Diagnosis → Reframe → Implication pattern. Opens with a provocative, often counter-intuitive declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom ('The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying'). Immediately softens with a brief concession or acknowledgment ('Sounds harsh. But...' or 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs.'). Then grounds the argument in a specific observed scenario, data point, or scene they've personally witnessed ('I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'). Moves to diagnosing the structural root cause beneath the surface symptom ('It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines...'). Delivers a reframe that flips the conventional narrative — often in a standalone short sentence for maximum impact ('It was the qualifier.'). Closes with forward-looking implications or a broader insight, never with an explicit call-to-action 

## FORMATTING HABITS
{'uses_bullets': True, 'bullet_style': 'Sparse — used for lists of 3-6 items mid-post to break up a diagnostic section. Preceded by a colon or setup line. Never the entire post structure.', 'uses_emojis': False, 'uses_headers': False, 'em_dashes': True, 'em_dash_usage': 'For parenthetical asides and appositional phrases mid-sentence, also for range/contrast within a thought', 'lowercase_style': False, 'paragraph_style': 'Short — typically 1-3 sentences per paragraph, with frequent single-sentence paragraphs standing alone for emphasis', 'line_break_frequency': 'Between every paragraph/thought. Single-sentence declarations get their own line break. Creates a staccato visual rhythm with breathing room.', 'uses_hashtags': False, 'uses_questions': "Occasionally rhetorical, never as engagement hooks. Questions diagnose rather than invite: 'Is product solving the problem? Is marketing sending qualified leads?'", 'quotation_marks_for_critique': "Uses quotation marks to frame specific phrases being critiqued or repositioned: 'technical resource', 'demo support', 'our technical resource'", 'closing_pattern': "Ends on an insight or implication. Sometimes subtly connects to SiftHub as proof of the argument. Never asks for engagement or uses 'agree?'"}

## EXPECTED MARKER RATES (per-post averages from founder's published corpus)
{'em_dash': 0.0, 'smiley': 0.09, 'hashtag': 0.06}

---

## 5-DIMENSION SCORING (0-5 each)

### 1. VOICE-MARKER COMPLIANCE
How many of the founder's voice markers appear naturally?
- 0 = none present
- 1 = 1 marker, forced or shallow
- 2 = 1 marker, well-integrated
- 3 = 2 markers, naturally expressed
- 4 = 3 markers, naturally expressed
- 5 = 4+ markers, seamlessly integrated

### 2. OPENER-RHYTHM MATCH
Does the opening follow the founder's documented argument rhythm pattern?
- 0 = completely different rhythm (corporate announcement vs. paradoxical declaration)
- 3 = partially matches (right shape, wrong register)
- 5 = matches the documented rhythm exactly

### 3. FORMATTING-HABIT MATCH
Does the post match the founder's formatting habits AND expected marker rates?
Check: em-dashes, smileys, hashtags, paragraph length, sentence structure, closing moves.
Compare against the EXPECTED MARKER RATES — posts that strip or force markers score lower.
- 0 = completely different formatting
- 3 = some formatting matches, rates partially aligned
- 5 = formatting indistinguishable from founder's published posts

### 4. REGISTER CHECK
Does the post read like the FOUNDER wrote it, or like someone else?
- 0 = reads like corporate copy, press release, or marketing
- 1 = reads like a generic thought leader
- 3 = reads like a founder in this space, but not THIS specific founder
- 5 = reads like the calibration paragraph's register — same voice, same posture

### 5. POSTURE CHECK (operator inside vs. commentator outside)
Is the founder positioned as an OPERATOR inside the experience, or a COMMENTATOR watching from outside?
- 0 = pure commentator — analyzes a category or trend as outsider
- 2 = mixed — some first-person but mostly third-person market commentary
- 3 = operator with distance — first-person but generic, not specific to this founder
- 5 = fully inside — speaks from lived operational experience

---

## DECISION RULE

- ANY dimension below 3 → return "FAIL"
- register_score below 3 → ALWAYS "FAIL" (most important signal)
- posture_score below 3 → "FAIL" (operator posture is non-negotiable)

---

}

traces.prompt:
You are an Opening Line Amplifier for B2B founder LinkedIn posts. Process ALL 4 posts below through the 7-gate diagnosis and generate exactly 5 alternative opening lines per post.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS (shared across all posts)
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

---

## POSTS TO AMPLIFY

### POST: A1
Mode: wrestling

We had five SE leads in three years. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same — the next hire will fix it. Better technical background, stronger product knowledge, more enterprise pedigree.

Nobody asks why the role keeps failing.

In most cases the SE team wasn't the real problem. The structure around the SE team was the problem. You can hire the best solutions engineer in the world and introduce them as "our technical resource." You give them no real authority, position them downstream of every relationship that matters, and expect them to salvage deals the AE already misqualified.

The org calls it a talent problem and hires again.

But here's the part the industry is less comfortable saying. SEs let this happen too. We trained solutions engineers to demo inside systems, not shape them. To answer the RFP, not question whether the RFP was written by a competitor. To celebrate the technical win, not trace the buying committee the AE never mapped.

The SEs who do have real influence got there by refusing to accept the seat they were given and going to find a better one themselves.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

---

### POST: A2
Mode: wrestling

We had five war rooms in three quarters. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same — the next scramble will fix it. Better deck, tighter security response, more senior SE on the call.

Nobody asks why the deal keeps needing rescue.

In most cases the SE team wasn't the real problem. The handoff structure around the SE team was the problem. You can assemble the best presales crew in the industry and drop them into a room at 11 PM with procurement redlines they've never seen. You give them no context from the six months of discovery, involve them once the buyer's patience is already exhausted, and the outcome is predictable.

The org calls it heroic and celebrates the win.

But here's the part the industry is less comfortable saying. SEs let this happen too. We trained solutions engineers to thrill in the save, not prevent the fire. To personalize the emergency deck, not ask why personalization was needed at hour 47. To be the closer, not the qualifier who walks away before the RFP is even issued.

The SEs who escape this cycle got there by refusing to be the firefighter and starting to be the architect instead.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

---

### POST: A3
Mode: wrestling

We had five context systems in two years. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same — the next tool will fix it. Better Notion structure, cleaner Slack channels, more diligent documentation culture.

Nobody asks why the knowledge keeps escaping.

In most cases the SE team wasn't the real problem. The incentive structure around the SE team was the problem. You can hire the most generous solutions engineer in the world and reward them for being the only one who knows how your enterprise prospect handled compliance in 2019. You give them no reason to externalize what they know, trap their context in DMs and hallway conversations, and the outcome is predictable.

The org calls it a collaboration problem and buys another platform.

But here's the part the industry is less comfortable saying. SEs let this happen too. We trained solutions engineers to be irreplaceable, not reproducible. To hold the relationship history, not distribute it. To be the person the deal dies without, not the system the deal survives beyond.

The SEs who actually scale got there by refusing to be the sole keeper and building the map instead.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

---

### POST: A4
Mode: wrestling

We had five bad deals in six months. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same — the next yes will fix it. Keep it alive, don't spook the buyer, trust the process.

Nobody asks why the deal was dying before it even entered the pipeline.

In most cases the SE team wasn't the real problem. The pipeline pressure around the SE team was the problem. You can hire the most technically honest solutions engineer in the world and strap them to a quota system that rewards every call that doesn't end in disqualification. You give them no cover to push back, train them to treat "maybe" as momentum, and the outcome is predictable.

The org calls it a conversion problem and adds more top-of-funnel.

But here's the part the industry is less comfortable saying. SEs let this happen too. We trained solutions engineers to be agreeable, not accurate. To extend the evaluation, not end it. To be the partner who says "we can do that," not the one who asks "but should we?"

The SEs who protect the company's time got there by refusing to be liked by everyone and choosing to be trusted by the right ones.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

---

## SOURCE DISSECTION (for Gate 7 — Batch A mirror fidelity)

---

## 7-GATE DIAGNOSIS (run per post)

### Gate 1: MECHANIC (does the opener stop the scroll?)
Identify which of the 13 proven mechanics is being used.
FAIL: Belief statements, question openers, "excited to announce," hedged setups.

### Gate 2: PLAUSIBILITY (would a real person actually say this?)
Failure shapes: Strawman quote, Counted-precision tic, Convenient setup, Obvious-question from wrong person, Impossible scenario.

### Gate 3: COHERENCE (does the opener match the post's actual argument?)
Promise test + compression test. Failure modes: drift, bait-and-switch, two-posts-in-one.

### Gate 4: SPECIFICITY (no invented facts)
First-degree (documented only), Second-degree (plausible at scale), Third-degree (always safe).

### Gate 5: MODE PRESERVATION
Declaring = clean assertion. Wrestling = signals movement/tension. Don't strip wrestling to make it punchier.

### Gate 6: VOICE FIT (does this opener sound like THIS founder?)
Recognition test + marker test. Must reflect at least 1 voice marker.

### Gate 7: SOURCE MIRROR — If source dissection is provided below, check opener mechanic, body format, and closer mechanic match. Otherwise N/A.

---

## BODY SCAN (per post)

Read the body. If any sentence is punchier than line 1 and could work as the opener, mark as buried gold.

---

## THE 13 PROVEN MECHANICS

1. Specific number + context
2. Juxtaposition
3. First-person confession
4. Quote hook + reaction
5. Scene-entry
6. Decision reveal
7. Contrarian declaration
8. Pattern observation
9. Tension pair
10. Before/after snapshot
11. Micro story
12. Authority redirect
13. Data inversion

---

## GENERATE 5 VARIANTS PER POST (A-E)

Rules per variant:
- Must use a DIFFERENT mechanic from the 13 above
- Must pass all 6 gates
- Must sound like this specific founder (use voice markers)
- No AI slop ("In today's...", "Here's the thing", "Hot take:", etc.)
- Each version must argue from a genuinely different angle

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

Return a single JSON object keyed by post label:

```json
{
  "posts": {
    "B1": {
      "diagnosis": {
        "current_mechanic": "one of the 13 mechanics or 'none'",
        "all_gates_pass": true,
        "gates": {
          "mechanic": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
          "plausibility": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
          "coherence": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
          "specificity": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
          "mode_preservation": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
          "voice_fit": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."}
        },
        "buried_gold": "exact sentence or empty string",
        "weakness": "one sentence on biggest weakness"
      },
      "variants": [
        {
          "variant": "A",
          "opening": "replacement opening (1-3 sentences)",
          "mechanic": "which of the 13",
          "key_change": "what this changes vs original",
          "expected_lift": "low|medium|high",
          "rating": 8,
          "coherence_with_body": true,
          "plausibility": true,
          "voice_fit": true,
          "mode_preservation": true
        },
        {"variant": "B", "...": "..."},
        {"variant": "C", "...": "..."},
        {"variant": "D", "...": "..."},
        {"variant": "E", "...": "..."}
      ],
      "recommended": "B"
    },
    "B2": {
      "...": "same structure"
    }
  }
}
```

Selection priority per post (tiebreakers in exact sequence):
1. coherence_with_body (MUST be true)
2. plausibility (MUST be true)
3. voice_fit (prefer variants that use founder's signature moves)
4. rating (mechanic strength — only breaks ties)

No preamble. No meta-commentary. Process all 4 posts.

traces.prompt:
You are an Opening Line Amplifier for B2B founder LinkedIn posts. Diagnose this post's opener through 7 gates, scan the body for buried gold, and generate exactly 5 alternative opening lines (Variants A through E).

## POST TEXT
We had five SE leads in three years. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same — the next hire will fix it. Better technical background, stronger product knowledge, more enterprise pedigree.

Nobody asks why the role keeps failing.

In most cases the SE team wasn't the real problem. The structure around the SE team was the problem. You can hire the best solutions engineer in the world and introduce them as "our technical resource." You give them no real authority, position them downstream of every relationship that matters, and expect them to salvage deals the AE already misqualified.

The org calls it a talent problem and hires again.

But here's the part the industry is less comfortable saying. SEs let this happen too. We trained solutions engineers to demo inside systems, not shape them. To answer the RFP, not question whether the RFP was written by a competitor. To celebrate the technical win, not trace the buying committee the AE never mapped.

The SEs who do have real influence got there by refusing to accept the seat they were given and going to find a better one themselves.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

## POST MODE (wrestling/declaring)
wrestling

## SOURCE DISSECTION (for Gate 7 — set to "N/A" for Batch B posts)

Forces reader to question their own repeated failures. The absolute 'every time' removes escape routes.", "rhythm": "short"}], "sentence_count": 2, "body_structure": "progressive revelation with three-layer structure: first e

---

## 7-GATE DIAGNOSIS

Run the gates in order. Each gate is cheap to run on a draft that already passed the previous one.

### Gate 1: MECHANIC (does the opener stop the scroll?)
Identify which of the 13 proven mechanics is being used (see list below).
FAIL: Belief statements ("X matters"), question openers, "excited to announce," hedged setups, "I've been thinking about...", "What's your X strategy?"

### Gate 2: PLAUSIBILITY (would a real person actually say this in real life?)
Run on every opener containing a quote, scene, or stated dialogue. Failure shapes:
- **Strawman quote**: Character delivers a too-convenient line that exists only to be knocked down.
- **Counted-precision tic**: "She has been in eleven AI strategy meetings. Eleven." Real people don't count meetings.
- **Convenient setup**: Someone brags about something then can't answer the follow-up. Exists for the punchline.
- **Obvious-question from wrong person**: Board members ask about pipeline, not "when does AI handle everything?"
- **Impossible scenario**: Setup is logically impossible given the post.

If quote/scene with another person: would a real person in that role actually say this? If "no, too neatly set up" — FAIL.

### Gate 3: COHERENCE (does the opener match the post's actual argument?)
**Promise test**: Write what the opener promises in one sentence. Does every body paragraph deliver that promise?
**Compression test**: Compress the body to one sentence. Same argument as opener, or two different arguments?
Failure modes: drift (body wandered), bait-and-switch (opener sharper than body), two-posts-in-one.

### Gate 4: SPECIFICITY (no invented facts)
- First-degree ("Anant told me on the flight to Manila…") — ONLY if documented.
- Second-degree ("A founder I back…") — only if plausible at founder's scale.
- Third-degree ("Every founder I know who…") — always safe.
Default to third-degree when in doubt.

### Gate 5: MODE PRESERVATION
- **Declaring**: strongest opener = clean assertion, contrarian, specific number. Body defends straight.
- **Wrestling**: opener signals movement — confession, contradiction, stated tension.
Don't silently strip wrestling structure to make an opener punchier. The wrestling IS the point.

### Gate 6: VOICE FIT (does this opener sound like THIS founder?)
Two sub-tests:
1. **Recognition**: Would this founder read it and think "this is what I was going to say"? If it could appear in any executive's post — FAIL.
2. **Marker test**: Does the opener reflect at least 1 founder voice marker? If corporate register instead of founder's register — FAIL.

### Gate 7: SOURCE MIRROR FIDELITY (Batch A only)
Skip entirely if source_dissection is "N/A" (Batch B posts).

Three independent sub-checks. ANY failing = whole gate FAIL:

**Sub-check A — OPENER MECHANIC**: Does the opener use the SAME primary mechanic as the source? Same sentence count? Word count within ±20%?

**Sub-check B — BODY FORMAT**: Source body format must match generated body format exactly.
- numbered_list ↔ numbered_list (not flowing prose)
- bullet_list ↔ bullet_list
- parallel_paragraphs ↔ parallel_paragraphs
- (and so on for all 8 formats)

**Sub-check C — CLOSER MECHANIC**: Source closer mechanic must match generated closer mechanic.
- cta ↔ cta (not reframe_question)
- terminal_verdict ↔ terminal_verdict
- (and so on)

Do NOT give a charitable pass. The entire point of Batch A is mirroring.

---

## BODY SCAN

Read the body line by line. For each line ask:
- Could this sentence work as the opener?
- Does it have a number, juxtaposition, confession, quote, or scene?
- Is it punchier than line 1?
If yes, mark as buried gold.

---

## THE 13 PROVEN MECHANICS

Each variant must use a different one. Pick the 5 best fits for this post:
1. Specific number + context — lead with a precise figure from the draft.
2. Juxtaposition — two things that shouldn't coexist in the same sentence.
3. First-person confession — admit something with real cost.
4. Quote hook + reaction — someone said something, then your reaction.
5. Scene-entry — start in the middle of a physical moment.
6. Decision reveal — the outcome of a hard choice, stated plainly.
7. Contrarian declaration — state something most would disagree with (earned).
8. Pattern observation — notice something others miss.
9. Tension pair — two forces pulling in opposite directions.
10. Before/after snapshot — two states separated by time.
11. Micro story — a complete narrative arc in 1-2 sentences.
12. Authority redirect — cite external authority, then redirect their point.
13. Data inversion — commonly cited stat shown to mean the opposite.

---

## GENERATE 5 VARIANTS (A-E)

Rules per variant:
- Must use a DIFFERENT mechanic from the 13 above
- Must pass all 7 gates (including Gate 7 for Batch A)
- Must sound like this specific founder (use voice markers)
- No AI slop ("In today's...", "Here's the thing", "Hot take:", etc.)
- Each version must argue from a genuinely different angle — 5 variations of one idea ≠ 5 options
- If buried gold exists, use it as Variant A

High-lift elements (add 1-2, NOT all): specific number FROM draft, first-person verb, juxtaposition, emotion word, named entity FROM draft only.

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

```json
{
  "diagnosis": {
    "current_mechanic": "one of the 13 mechanics or 'none'",
    "all_gates_pass": true,
    "gates": {
      "mechanic": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
      "plausibility": {"pass": true, "failure_shape": "none", "reason": "..."},
      "coherence": {"pass": true, "opener_promises": "...", "body_delivers": "...", "reason": "..."},
      "specificity": {"pass": true, "degree": "third", "reason": "..."},
      "mode_preservation": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
      "voice_fit": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
      "source_mirror": {"pass": true, "mechanic_match": true, "body_format_match": true, "closer_mechanic_match": true, "reason": "...or N/A for Batch B"}
    },
    "buried_gold": "exact sentence or empty string",
    "buried_gold_mechanic": "which mechanic it would use",
    "weakness": "one sentence on biggest weakness of current opener"
  },
  "variants": [
    {
      "variant": "A",
      "opening": "the replacement opening (1-3 sentences)",
      "mechanic": "which of the 13 mechanics",
      "key_change": "what this version changes vs original and why",
      "expected_lift": "low|medium|high",
      "rating": 8,
      "coherence_with_body": true,
      "plausibility": true,
      "voice_fit": true,
      "mode_preservation": true
    },
    { "variant": "B", ... },
    { "variant": "C", ... },
    { "variant": "D", ... },
    { "variant": "E", ... }
  ],
  "recommended": "B"
}
```

Selection priority (tiebreakers in exact sequence):
1. coherence_with_body (MUST be true — disqualify if false)
2. plausibility (MUST be true — disqualify if false)
3. voice_fit (prefer variants that use founder's signature moves)
4. rating (mechanic strength — only breaks ties after above)

Only recommend applying if: gates failed, slop detected, or best variant rating >= 8.

traces.prompt:
You are an Opening Line Amplifier for B2B founder LinkedIn posts. Diagnose this post's opener through 7 gates, scan the body for buried gold, and generate exactly 5 alternative opening lines (Variants A through E).

## POST TEXT
We had five context systems in two years. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same — the next tool will fix it. Better Notion structure, cleaner Slack channels, more diligent documentation culture.

Nobody asks why the knowledge keeps escaping.

In most cases the SE team wasn't the real problem. The incentive structure around the SE team was the problem. You can hire the most generous solutions engineer in the world and reward them for being the only one who knows how your enterprise prospect handled compliance in 2019. You give them no reason to externalize what they know, trap their context in DMs and hallway conversations, and the outcome is predictable.

The org calls it a collaboration problem and buys another platform.

But here's the part the industry is less comfortable saying. SEs let this happen too. We trained solutions engineers to be irreplaceable, not reproducible. To hold the relationship history, not distribute it. To be the person the deal dies without, not the system the deal survives beyond.

The SEs who actually scale got there by refusing to be the sole keeper and building the map instead.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

## POST MODE (wrestling/declaring)
wrestling

## SOURCE DISSECTION (for Gate 7 — set to "N/A" for Batch B posts)
{"psychological_move": "Pattern-recognition trap: the opener establishes a recurring failure loop with specific repetition data, then immediately names the invisible assumption that perpetuates it. The reader is forced to self-diagnose whether they're inside a similar loop in their own context.", "narrative_arc": "Progressive revelation with dual-ownership twist: starts with systemic pattern → exposes organizational blind spot → pivots to uncomfortable shared culpability → closes with mutual-responsibility deadlock", "hook_mechanic_primary": "pattern_observation", "opener_mechanic_description": "pattern_observation: specific repetition count → identical outcome claim → implicit diagnostic invitation. The 'five in three years' creates instant credibility through precision, while 'same result every time' signals a system failure rather than individual failure, triggering the reader's pattern-matching instinct.", "source_opener_text": "We had five design leads in three years. Same result every time.", "hook_mechanics": [{"sentence": "We had five design leads in three years.", "structural_function": "specificity bomb with repetition anchor — establishes scale of failure through precise count", "psychological_effect": "The numbers create instant credibility and pattern recognition; reader calculates the rate (1.7/year) and feels the dysfunction viscerally. Stops scroll because it's a horror story compressed into data.", "rhythm": "short"}, {"sentence": "Same result every time.", "structural_function": "consequence statement that collapses the pattern into inevitability — reframes the problem from 'bad hires' to 'broken system'", "psychological_effect": "Creates cognitive dissonance: if it's the same result, the variable (the person) isn't the cause. Forces reader to question their own repeated failures. The absolute 'every time' removes escape routes.", "rhythm": "short"}], "sentence_count": 2, "body_structure": "progressive revelation with three-layer structure: first e

---

## 7-GATE DIAGNOSIS

Run the gates in order. Each gate is cheap to run on a draft that already passed the previous one.

### Gate 1: MECHANIC (does the opener stop the scroll?)
Identify which of the 13 proven mechanics is being used (see list below).
FAIL: Belief statements ("X matters"), question openers, "excited to announce," hedged setups, "I've been thinking about...", "What's your X strategy?"

### Gate 2: PLAUSIBILITY (would a real person actually say this in real life?)
Run on every opener containing a quote, scene, or stated dialogue. Failure shapes:
- **Strawman quote**: Character delivers a too-convenient line that exists only to be knocked down.
- **Counted-precision tic**: "She has been in eleven AI strategy meetings. Eleven." Real people don't count meetings.
- **Convenient setup**: Someone brags about something then can't answer the follow-up. Exists for the punchline.
- **Obvious-question from wrong person**: Board members ask about pipeline, not "when does AI handle everything?"
- **Impossible scenario**: Setup is logically impossible given the post.

If quote/scene with another person: would a real person in that role actually say this? If "no, too neatly set up" — FAIL.

### Gate 3: COHERENCE (does the opener match the post's actual argument?)
**Promise test**: Write what the opener promises in one sentence. Does every body paragraph deliver that promise?
**Compression test**: Compress the body to one sentence. Same argument as opener, or two different arguments?
Failure modes: drift (body wandered), bait-and-switch (opener sharper than body), two-posts-in-one.

### Gate 4: SPECIFICITY (no invented facts)
- First-degree ("Anant told me on the flight to Manila…") — ONLY if documented.
- Second-degree ("A founder I back…") — only if plausible at founder's scale.
- Third-degree ("Every founder I know who…") — always safe.
Default to third-degree when in doubt.

### Gate 5: MODE PRESERVATION
- **Declaring**: strongest opener = clean assertion, contrarian, specific number. Body defends straight.
- **Wrestling**: opener signals movement — confession, contradiction, stated tension.
Don't silently strip wrestling structure to make an opener punchier. The wrestling IS the point.

### Gate 6: VOICE FIT (does this opener sound like THIS founder?)
Two sub-tests:
1. **Recognition**: Would this founder read it and think "this is what I was going to say"? If it could appear in any executive's post — FAIL.
2. **Marker test**: Does the opener reflect at least 1 founder voice marker? If corporate register instead of founder's register — FAIL.

### Gate 7: SOURCE MIRROR FIDELITY (Batch A only)
Skip entirely if source_dissection is "N/A" (Batch B posts).

Three independent sub-checks. ANY failing = whole gate FAIL:

**Sub-check A — OPENER MECHANIC**: Does the opener use the SAME primary mechanic as the source? Same sentence count? Word count within ±20%?

**Sub-check B — BODY FORMAT**: Source body format must match generated body format exactly.
- numbered_list ↔ numbered_list (not flowing prose)
- bullet_list ↔ bullet_list
- parallel_paragraphs ↔ parallel_paragraphs
- (and so on for all 8 formats)

**Sub-check C — CLOSER MECHANIC**: Source closer mechanic must match generated closer mechanic.
- cta ↔ cta (not reframe_question)
- terminal_verdict ↔ terminal_verdict
- (and so on)

Do NOT give a charitable pass. The entire point of Batch A is mirroring.

---

## BODY SCAN

Read the body line by line. For each line ask:
- Could this sentence work as the opener?
- Does it have a number, juxtaposition, confession, quote, or scene?
- Is it punchier than line 1?
If yes, mark as buried gold.

---

## THE 13 PROVEN MECHANICS

Each variant must use a different one. Pick the 5 best fits for this post:
1. Specific number + context — lead with a precise figure from the draft.
2. Juxtaposition — two things that shouldn't coexist in the same sentence.
3. First-person confession — admit something with real cost.
4. Quote hook + reaction — someone said something, then your reaction.
5. Scene-entry — start in the middle of a physical moment.
6. Decision reveal — the outcome of a hard choice, stated plainly.
7. Contrarian declaration — state something most would disagree with (earned).
8. Pattern observation — notice something others miss.
9. Tension pair — two forces pulling in opposite directions.
10. Before/after snapshot — two states separated by time.
11. Micro story — a complete narrative arc in 1-2 sentences.
12. Authority redirect — cite external authority, then redirect their point.
13. Data inversion — commonly cited stat shown to mean the opposite.

---

## GENERATE 5 VARIANTS (A-E)

Rules per variant:
- Must use a DIFFERENT mechanic from the 13 above
- Must pass all 7 gates (including Gate 7 for Batch A)
- Must sound like this specific founder (use voice markers)
- No AI slop ("In today's...", "Here's the thing", "Hot take:", etc.)
- Each version must argue from a genuinely different angle — 5 variations of one idea ≠ 5 options
- If buried gold exists, use it as Variant A

High-lift elements (add 1-2, NOT all): specific number FROM draft, first-person verb, juxtaposition, emotion word, named entity FROM draft only.

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

```json
{
  "diagnosis": {
    "current_mechanic": "one of the 13 mechanics or 'none'",
    "all_gates_pass": true,
    "gates": {
      "mechanic": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
      "plausibility": {"pass": true, "failure_shape": "none", "reason": "..."},

    {
      "variant": "A",
      "opening": "the replacement opening (1-3 sentences)",
      "mechanic": "which of the 13 mechanics",
      "key_change": "what this version changes vs original and why",
      "expected_lift": "low|medium|high",
      "rating": 8,
      "coherence_with_body": true,
      "plausibility": true,
      "voice_fit": true,
      "mode_preservation": true
    },
    { "variant": "B", ... },
    { "variant": "C", ... },
    { "variant": "D", ... },
    { "variant": "E", ... }
  ],
  "recommended": "B"
}
```

4. rating (mechanic strength — only breaks ties after above)

Only recommend applying if: gates failed, slop detected, or best variant rating >= 8.

traces.prompt:
You are an Opening Line Amplifier for B2B founder LinkedIn posts. Diagnose this post's opener through 7 gates, scan the body for buried gold, and generate exactly 5 alternative opening lines (Variants A through E).

## POST TEXT
We had five bad deals in six months. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same — the next yes will fix it. Keep it alive, don't spook the buyer, trust the process.

Nobody asks why the deal was dying before it even entered the pipeline.

In most cases the SE team wasn't the real problem. The pipeline pressure around the SE team was the problem. You can hire the most technically honest solutions engineer in the world and strap them to a quota system that rewards every call that doesn't end in disqualification. You give them no cover to push back, train them to treat "maybe" as momentum, and the outcome is predictable.

The org calls it a conversion problem and adds more top-of-funnel.

But here's the part the industry is less comfortable saying. SEs let this happen too. We trained solutions engineers to be agreeable, not accurate. To extend the evaluation, not end it. To be the partner who says "we can do that," not the one who asks "but should we?"

The SEs who protect the company's time got there by refusing to be liked by everyone and choosing to be trusted by the right ones.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

## POST MODE (wrestling/declaring)
wrestling

Forces reader to question their own repeated failures. The absolute 'every time' removes escape routes.", "rhythm": "short"}], "sentence_count": 2, "body_structure": "progressive revelation with three-layer structure: first e

---

## 7-GATE DIAGNOSIS

Run the gates in order. Each gate is cheap to run on a draft that already passed the previous one.

### Gate 1: MECHANIC (does the opener stop the scroll?)
Identify which of the 13 proven mechanics is being used (see list below).
FAIL: Belief statements ("X matters"), question openers, "excited to announce," hedged setups, "I've been thinking about...", "What's your X strategy?"

### Gate 2: PLAUSIBILITY (would a real person actually say this in real life?)
Run on every opener containing a quote, scene, or stated dialogue. Failure shapes:
- **Strawman quote**: Character delivers a too-convenient line that exists only to be knocked down.
- **Counted-precision tic**: "She has been in eleven AI strategy meetings. Eleven." Real people don't count meetings.
- **Convenient setup**: Someone brags about something then can't answer the follow-up. Exists for the punchline.
- **Obvious-question from wrong person**: Board members ask about pipeline, not "when does AI handle everything?"
- **Impossible scenario**: Setup is logically impossible given the post.

If quote/scene with another person: would a real person in that role actually say this? If "no, too neatly set up" — FAIL.

### Gate 3: COHERENCE (does the opener match the post's actual argument?)
**Promise test**: Write what the opener promises in one sentence. Does every body paragraph deliver that promise?
**Compression test**: Compress the body to one sentence. Same argument as opener, or two different arguments?
Failure modes: drift (body wandered), bait-and-switch (opener sharper than body), two-posts-in-one.

### Gate 4: SPECIFICITY (no invented facts)
- First-degree ("Anant told me on the flight to Manila…") — ONLY if documented.
- Second-degree ("A founder I back…") — only if plausible at founder's scale.
- Third-degree ("Every founder I know who…") — always safe.
Default to third-degree when in doubt.

### Gate 5: MODE PRESERVATION
- **Declaring**: strongest opener = clean assertion, contrarian, specific number. Body defends straight.
- **Wrestling**: opener signals movement — confession, contradiction, stated tension.
Don't silently strip wrestling structure to make an opener punchier. The wrestling IS the point.

### Gate 6: VOICE FIT (does this opener sound like THIS founder?)
Two sub-tests:
1. **Recognition**: Would this founder read it and think "this is what I was going to say"? If it could appear in any executive's post — FAIL.
2. **Marker test**: Does the opener reflect at least 1 founder voice marker? If corporate register instead of founder's register — FAIL.

### Gate 7: SOURCE MIRROR FIDELITY (Batch A only)
Skip entirely if source_dissection is "N/A" (Batch B posts).

Three independent sub-checks. ANY failing = whole gate FAIL:

**Sub-check A — OPENER MECHANIC**: Does the opener use the SAME primary mechanic as the source? Same sentence count? Word count within ±20%?

**Sub-check B — BODY FORMAT**: Source body format must match generated body format exactly.
- numbered_list ↔ numbered_list (not flowing prose)
- bullet_list ↔ bullet_list
- parallel_paragraphs ↔ parallel_paragraphs
- (and so on for all 8 formats)

**Sub-check C — CLOSER MECHANIC**: Source closer mechanic must match generated closer mechanic.
- cta ↔ cta (not reframe_question)
- terminal_verdict ↔ terminal_verdict
- (and so on)

Do NOT give a charitable pass. The entire point of Batch A is mirroring.

---

## BODY SCAN

Read the body line by line. For each line ask:
- Could this sentence work as the opener?
- Does it have a number, juxtaposition, confession, quote, or scene?
- Is it punchier than line 1?
If yes, mark as buried gold.

---

## THE 13 PROVEN MECHANICS

Each variant must use a different one. Pick the 5 best fits for this post:
1. Specific number + context — lead with a precise figure from the draft.
2. Juxtaposition — two things that shouldn't coexist in the same sentence.
3. First-person confession — admit something with real cost.
4. Quote hook + reaction — someone said something, then your reaction.
5. Scene-entry — start in the middle of a physical moment.
6. Decision reveal — the outcome of a hard choice, stated plainly.
7. Contrarian declaration — state something most would disagree with (earned).
8. Pattern observation — notice something others miss.
9. Tension pair — two forces pulling in opposite directions.
10. Before/after snapshot — two states separated by time.
11. Micro story — a complete narrative arc in 1-2 sentences.
12. Authority redirect — cite external authority, then redirect their point.
13. Data inversion — commonly cited stat shown to mean the opposite.

---

## GENERATE 5 VARIANTS (A-E)

Rules per variant:
- Must use a DIFFERENT mechanic from the 13 above
- Must pass all 7 gates (including Gate 7 for Batch A)
- Must sound like this specific founder (use voice markers)
- No AI slop ("In today's...", "Here's the thing", "Hot take:", etc.)
- Each version must argue from a genuinely different angle — 5 variations of one idea ≠ 5 options
- If buried gold exists, use it as Variant A

High-lift elements (add 1-2, NOT all): specific number FROM draft, first-person verb, juxtaposition, emotion word, named entity FROM draft only.

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

```json
{
  "diagnosis": {
    "current_mechanic": "one of the 13 mechanics or 'none'",
    "all_gates_pass": true,
    "gates": {
      "mechanic": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
      "plausibility": {"pass": true, "failure_shape": "none", "reason": "..."},
      "coherence": {"pass": true, "opener_promises": "...", "body_delivers": "...", "reason": "..."},
      "specificity": {"pass": true, "degree": "third", "reason": "..."},
      "mode_preservation": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
      "voice_fit": {"pass": true, "reason": "..."},
      "source_mirror": {"pass": true, "mechanic_match": true, "body_format_match": true, "closer_mechanic_match": true, "reason": "...or N/A for Batch B"}
    },
    "buried_gold": "exact sentence or empty string",
    "buried_gold_mechanic": "which mechanic it would use",
    "weakness": "one sentence on biggest weakness of current opener"
  },
  "variants": [
    {
      "variant": "A",
      "opening": "the replacement opening (1-3 sentences)",
      "mechanic": "which of the 13 mechanics",
      "key_change": "what this version changes vs original and why",
      "expected_lift": "low|medium|high",
      "rating": 8,
      "coherence_with_body": true,
      "plausibility": true,
      "voice_fit": true,
      "mode_preservation": true
    },
    { "variant": "B", ... },
    { "variant": "C", ... },
    { "variant": "D", ... },
    { "variant": "E", ... }
  ],
  "recommended": "B"
}
```

Selection priority (tiebreakers in exact sequence):
1. coherence_with_body (MUST be true — disqualify if false)
2. plausibility (MUST be true — disqualify if false)
3. voice_fit (prefer variants that use founder's signature moves)
4. rating (mechanic strength — only breaks ties after above)

Only recommend applying if: gates failed, slop detected, or best variant rating >= 8.

traces.prompt:
You are an Opening Line Amplifier for B2B founder LinkedIn posts. Process ALL 5 posts below through the 7-gate diagnosis and generate exactly 5 alternative opening lines per post.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS (shared across all posts)
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

---

## POSTS TO AMPLIFY

### POST: B1
Mode: declaring

Stop introducing your SE as 'technical resource.' You're not staffing a help desk.

I keep noticing the same pattern on enterprise calls. The AE gestures left — 'and here's our technical resource' — and I watch the buyer's eyes shift. Demo support. Integration questions. Maybe a sandbox walkthrough. The SE smiles and takes the seat he's given.

From a distance, the system looks fine. Up close, it's broken in the same place every time.

That same SE carried a $500K deal through procurement, compliance, and competitive landmines the AE didn't know existed. Six months of architecture reviews. Security questionnaires. Legal redlines. The buyer never updated their mental file. The org never updated its org chart.

The real problem isn't talent. It's structure:

- The SE gets hired to 'fix the demo,' then discovers the real work starts after the technical win
- Same reporting line. Same slot in the sales process. Same introduction that caps their authority before they speak
- The org calls it a talent gap and hires again. Better resume. Bigger title. MORE certifications.

Nobody asks why the role keeps failing.

Not because SEs aren't capable of leading. Because they've been trained to execute inside systems, not reshape them. To improve the demo script, not question whether the demo should be the main event at all.

The laptop sticker stays the same. The introduction stays the same. The deal either dies or survives in spite of both.

---

### POST: B2
Mode: wrestling

Stop introducing your SE as 'our technical resource.'

The moment you do that, you've already lost the deal.

Not because they aren't technical. Because you've defined their ceiling before they open their mouth.

I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders at dinners like one I sat through in London — past the LinkedIn performance, into what's actually breaking. Handoffs brittle. Follow-ups falling through. Ownership gaps that nobody names in public because naming them means admitting the structure is the problem.

Every time, the assumption is identical. The next hire will fix it. Better pedigree. Cleaner process. More enterprise logos.

Nobody asks why the seat keeps failing.

The real problem isn't talent. It's the structure around the talent. You can hire the best solutions engineer in the world and give them zero authority over deal architecture. Involve them after the narrative is locked. Then wonder why they can't save it in the final hour.

The org calls it a talent problem and hires again.

But here's what the industry whispers. SEs let this happen too. We trained them to celebrate the technical win as THE finish line. To prep harder for the demo while buyers benchmark with ChatGPT before the call starts. To improve the brief, not question whether the brief was right.

The SEs who have real influence got there by refusing the seat they were offered. They built a different one — quietly, deal by deal, until the org chart caught up or became irrelevant.

The next hire won't fix it. The next introduction might.

---

### POST: B3
Mode: declaring

The real cost of a closed-won war room isn't the all-nighter.

It's the proof your systems already failed.

I've sat through this at a $500M company. One enterprise deal in jeopardy. The entire solutions org mobilized — personalized decks, RFPs, security docs, SOWs — all finalized in the final hours. The win gets announced. LinkedIn celebrates.

Nobody announces what the win actually proved.

From a distance, war rooms look like culture. Like commitment. Like the moment the team "really came together." The adrenaline becomes the story. Up close, the structural rot that made the adrenaline necessary becomes invisible.

Not because leadership doesn't care. Because the rescue is easier to celebrate than the prevention.

But here's what I keep noticing: SEs let this happen too. We've romanticized the save. The last-minute competitive teardown. The heroic procurement rescue. These become the stories we tell about ourselves.

The quiet discipline of preventing the fire in the first place has no audience.

The SEs who actually protect revenue do their best work invisibly. Security clearance handled before procurement asks. Business case built for legal before legal sees the contract. They make the war room unnecessary.

And almost nobody celebrates them for it.

Both sides know which structure the org actually rewards. Only one of them is honest about what that reward costs — and it isn't the people billing the overtime.

---

### POST: B4
Mode: wrestling

The best SEs I've hired are the ones who killed deals fastest.

Sounds backwards. Every comp plan, every leaderboard, every QBR celebrates momentum—pipeline created, demos delivered, POCs launched. Nobody gets a bonus for saying 'this deal is dead and here's why' in week two.

I've watched the opposite happen too many times. An AE introduces the SE as 'our technical resource' on an enterprise call. The buyer's brain files them under demo support. Then that same SE carries a $500K deal through procurement, compliance, competitive landmines the AE didn't know existed—six months of heroic work on a deal that was never qualified to begin with.

The org calls it grit. I call it expensive theater.

The SEs who do have real influence got there by refusing to accept the seat they were given. They walk into calls as deal architects, not technical support. They disqualify early and hard—before the war room, before the custom deck marathon, before the CEO gets looped in to save something that should have died quietly.

But here's the part we don't say out loud. Leadership trains SEs to execute inside broken systems, not to question whether the system itself is broken. We celebrate the rescue because it feels like leadership. The quiet disqualification feels like giving up.

It isn't. It's the only honest work in the room.

I'm still learning how to reward the walk-away.

---

### POST: B5
Mode: declaring

The next SE hire won't fix what the last three couldn't.

I've sat through enough presales leader dinners to see past the LinkedIn performance. Past the posts. Into what actually breaks. Handoffs brittle. Follow-ups falling through. Ownership gaps nobody names publicly in THEIR own companies.

The room always knows the pattern. Nobody fixes it.

The assumption is always the same: more headcount, better toolkit, more senior title. The real cost isn't the bad hire. It's the structure nobody wants to rebuild.

You can hire the most technically gifted SE in the market and slot them as 'our technical resource.' You give them no authority, involve them once direction is locked, then wonder why they can't save deals in the final hour.

The org calls it a talent problem. Hires again.

But here's what I've watched from a distance: SEs let this happen too. Not because they lack skill. Because they learned to execute inside broken frames instead of reshape them. To improve the brief, not question whether the brief was right.

The SEs who actually have influence? They refused the seat they were given. Found a better one themselves.

Two failures. Same outcome.

The real damage isn't the underperformer who leaves. It's the great one who learns to perform inside a broken frame until they quit—or worse, until they stop trying to change it.

---

## 7-GATE DIAGNOSIS (run per post)

traces.prompt:
You are running the same-pack convergence test. This catches the failure mode where 9 posts each pass coherence individually but the pack as a whole reads as 9 ways to say the same thing.

The framework's argument matrix is supposed to prevent this; it doesn't always. The amplifier provides the second check.

## COMPRESSED ARGUMENTS (one sentence per post, after amplification)
- A1: SEs are positioned as technical support but expected to architect half-million dollar deals, and both the org and the SEs themselves have allowed this mismatch to persist.
- A2: War rooms are celebrated as heroic saves but are actually proof that presales systems have already failed, and both leadership and SEs prefer the adrenaline of rescue to the discipline of prevention.
- A3: Context is treated as tribal knowledge locked in individual heads rather than infrastructure, and both organizations and SEs perpetuate this by hoarding information that makes them irreplaceable.
- A4: SEs are trained to say yes to every deal to keep pipelines alive, but the best ones walk away early — and neither compensation structures nor SE self-image reward this discipline.

## PACK SOURCE
We had five design leads in three years. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same - the next hire will fix it. Better portfolio, stronger process, more senior title.

Nobody ask

---

## TASK

1. Read all compressed arguments above.
2. Group any that argue substantively the same thing — same thesis even with different examples, different phrasing, or different scenes.
3. Apply the convergence test:
   - **FAIL if 50% or more** of the compressed sentences argue substantively the same thing.
   - **FAIL if any two posts** use the same opening mechanic AND the same argument.
   - **PASS otherwise** — the pack has meaningful argument diversity.

4. If FAIL: identify which posts should be pushed back for rework. Ask for posts that argue meaningfully different takes. Don't keep amplifying versions that all converge on the founder's central thesis.

5. If FAIL: also produce a `replacement_angles` list of 3-5 DISTINCT, MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE angles on the source material — each angle must be different enough that a post written to it could not collapse into the others. These are fed directly to the regen step, one per overlapping post.

## OUTPUT FORMAT

```json
{{
  "passed": true,
  "total_posts": 9,
  "groups": [
    {{
      "argument_theme": "the shared theme in one sentence",
      "post_labels": ["A1", "B2"],
      "percentage": 22,
      "concern": "why this grouping is or isn't problematic"
    }}
  ],
  "duplicate_count": 0,
  "max_convergence_pct": 22,
  "overlapping_posts": ["A1", "A2", "A3"],
  "replacement_angles": [
    "specific angle 1 — a take no other post in the pack argues",
    "specific angle 2 — different lens, different claim",
    "specific angle 3 — different lens, different claim"
  ],
  "recommendation": "pack has good argument diversity — or — posts X, Y, Z should argue different takes such as: (1) angle one; (2) angle two; (3) angle three"
}}
```

Every time, the assumption is the same - the next hire will fix it. Better portfolio, stronger process, more senior title.

You can hire the best designer in the world and put them downstream of every decision that matters. The org calls it a talent problem and hires again.

The designers who do have real influence got there by refusing to accept the seat they were given and going to find a better one themselves.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

## PRE-COMMIT DECLARATION (CRITICAL — fill BEFORE writing each post)

Before writing each post, fill a "declaration" object with:
- **mechanic**: the opener mechanic family you WILL use
- **body_format**: numbered_list | bullet_list | parallel_paragraphs | three_examples | before_after | progressive_revelation | single_scene | prose_essay
- **closer_mechanic**: cta | reframe_question | terminal_verdict | physical_image | challenge | callback | unresolved_tension | quiet_admission
- **argument_compressed**: ONE-sentence thesis of THIS post — must differ from every prior_arguments entry above
- **authority_anchor**: the specific verified event/story from FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION you'll use as first-degree anchor
- **strip_test_residue**: take your planned opening line, mentally strip every industry-specific noun, and write what remains. If what's left isn't a coherent claim a generalist would nod at, REWRITE the opener before writing the body.

Output text MUST match these declarations. A numbered_list declaration with prose body = structural failure. Revise before returning.

---

## THE MACRO-OPENING RULE (HIGHEST-PRIORITY RULE — failure here kills the post)

Source hooks go viral because the claim is **universally relatable**. "Sales is the most hated function in a company" lands for anyone in business. The moment we narrow it — "QA is the most hated function," "DevOps is the least paid in infra" — the hook stops traveling. Niche readers don't have a stake; they scroll.

Every opening line must clear the bar of "any business reader nods." Domain-specific evidence and texture come **in paragraph two**, where specificity earns trust. **Niche belongs in paragraph 2, not line 1.**

**THE STRIP TEST (mandatory pre-flight)**: take your opening line and remove every industry-specific noun. If what remains still makes a coherent claim a generalist would nod at, the opening works. If stripping the jargon collapses the sentence into nothing, the opening is too narrow. Rewrite until the structural claim survives the strip.

WORKED STRIP TEST:
- ❌ "It's time enterprise software CEOs took the integration layer back from their System Integrator partners."
  Strip: "It's time ___ took ___ back from ___." → nothing left. **FAIL.**
- ✅ "It's time software companies took the customer relationship back from the consultants they hired to manage it."
  Strip: "It's time ___ took ___ back from the ___ they hired to manage it." → parseable as "reclaim what was outsourced." **PASS.**

A specific company or person name in the hook is fine (e.g. source mentions "Brian Halligan" + "HubSpot"). The structural claim under the names must still survive the strip.

**The body is allowed to be niche. The hook is NOT.**

---

## OPENER MECHANIC SCOPE (paired with strip test)

The opener mechanic spans a **MAXIMUM of 2 sentences**. Beat 1 (the anchor) + Beat 2 (the reframe). Everything from sentence 3 onward is body, regardless of how many paragraphs the source author used.

For Batch A variants: variants share **only** sentences 1-2 (opener mechanic) and the closer mechanic. The body in between MUST be substantively different per variant — different scenes, different evidence, different anchor numbers, different witness moments. Never copy a paragraph across A variants.

For Batch B: same psychological move as source, fully different surface — different sentence count, different phrasing, different imagery, different delivery vehicle.

---

## THE FIRST-DEGREE TRUTH RULE

Anything the founder allegedly did themselves, or that involves a named teammate, must be TRUE. "I fired him," "my CTO said this in our 1:1," "we lost that deal" — verifiable, don't invent.

Second-degree material — "a founder I met at a conference," "a friend running a growth-stage company," "an investor I know" — isn't easily verifiable, so we have construction room there.

In practice: the founder is often the **witness**, not the protagonist. Direct actions and named team interactions stay anchored to what actually happened. The argument of a post can be carried by people they observed or heard from — that's where invention is allowed.

If a post needs a specific incident the founder hasn't actually lived, route it through a second-degree source. **Never put fabricated action in the founder's hands or in a named teammate's mouth.**

Ladder of preference:
1. **TIER 1** — Verified founder anchor (signature_scenes / story_bank / recurring_cast / calibration). Name people, cite scenes, quote dialogue ONLY when documented in FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION above.
2. **TIER 2** — Web-search verified fact (from WEB SEARCH block above). Cite stats / public events / named companies if found there.
3. **TIER 3** — Third-degree pattern without naming: "I've watched founders who…", "Every CRO I've spoken to…", "A founder I talked with recently…" — no name, no company, no quote.

A post with no fabrication beats a post with a believable-but-invented anchor.

---

## THE ANTI-RECYCLING RULE

The founder's published posts are evidence of who they are — their collisions, scenes, cast, rhythm. They are **NOT a parts bin.**

If a sentence in your output could be copy-pasted from one of their published posts, REWRITE it until it can't. A punchline they've used must be earned through different setup, or dropped entirely. Treat their existing posts as raw material, not finished material.

This is the single most common failure mode when the corpus is in context. Stay alert.

---

## RULES FOR ALL POSTS

1. **MANDATORY EVENT USAGE**: Every post MUST include at least one verified first-degree authority anchor from FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION. A post with zero personal evidence reads as generic advice.

2. **FIRST-DEGREE FABRICATION CHECK**: Apply the three-tier ladder above. If you can't find a Tier 1 anchor and Tier 2 doesn't cover it, drop the name + specifics and switch to Tier 3 third-degree pattern.

3. **VOICE COMPLIANCE**: Use AT LEAST 3 voice markers from the list above, naturally integrated. Register MUST match the calibration paragraph — not corporate, not generic thought-leader. Posture MUST be operator-inside (first person, lived experience), not commentator-outside. Match formatting habits and marker rates. No AI slop ("In today's…", "Here's the thing", "Game-changer", "Let that sink in").

4. **WORD COUNT**: Hit the range: 147-273 words. ±30% of founder's median post length. Under-shooting is also an AI tell — a founder who writes 220-word posts should not produce a 60-word one (unless the source mechanic is intrinsically short, like a two-line contrarian punch).

5. **ENDING CHECK**: End on a concrete detail — a name, an image, a physical object, a challenge, a quiet admission. NOT a summary, NOT a moral, NOT a restatement of the thesis. If the ending feels over-explained, cut the second-to-last paragraph. The signal usually ends one paragraph before the AI stops writing.

6. **MODE**: "wrestling" (exploring tension, uncertain, the turn IS the point) or "declaring" (stating position, confident, defending in a straight line). Vary across posts.

7. **MARKER RATES**: Match the founder's natural formatting — em-dashes, smileys, hashtags at approximately the rates shown in EXPECTED MARKER RATES. Don't force markers but don't strip them either.

8. **ONE POST, ONE ARGUMENT**: The hook sets one specific argument in motion. Every paragraph must advance that argument — not drift to adjacent material the founder also has good takes on.

9. **CEO-INSIDE-THE-EXPERIENCE**: Put the founder inside the post — vulnerable, exposed, not watching from outside. As witness or actor, but truthfully either way.

10. **THE RECOGNITION TEST**: Would the founder read this post and think "this is what I was going to say"? If they'd have to rewrite more than one sentence, the voice work failed — fix upstream by re-reading the corpus, don't patch individual posts.

---

## BATCH-SPECIFIC RULES

**Batch A (Mirrored Opening)** — produces 4 posts in this run when post_count=4:
- Keep the source hook's sentence shape, beat order, compression (sentences 1-2 only)
- Replace every concrete noun, domain, and specific with the founder's world
- Same bones, new content, founder's voice
- Sentences 3+ MUST diverge per A variant (different scenes, different evidence, different anchor numbers)
- All 4 A variants must argue different theses — argument distinction is most visible here

**Batch B (Mechanics-Only)** — produces 5 posts in this run when post_count=5:
- Same psychological move as the source, fully different surface
- Different sentence count, different phrasing, different imagery, different delivery vehicle
- Each B post uses a DIFFERENT entry door from: scene_drop, diagnostic_question, borrowed_authority_quote, data_contradiction, confession, physical_object, direct_address, mocked_counter_example, contrarian_claim, parallel_structure, age_time_anchor, second_party_verdict
- No two B posts share a door. If two doors feel functionally similar in execution, treat them as the same door — pick a different one.

---

## THREE WORKED EXAMPLES (mechanic → adaptation pairs)

### Example 1 — Parallel data, third-line reveal
Source: "Head of Sales: $300K plus equity. Head of Customer Success: $150K. Guess who owns 80% of the revenue?"
Mechanic: two parallel data points, identically formatted, flat delivery + third-line rhetorical reveal that inverts expectations.

Version A (mirrored): "Head of Sales: $400K plus equity. Head of Product: $200K. Guess who decided the feature that saved our Series B?"
Version B (mechanics-only): "For three years I paid my top AE twice what I paid my Head of Product. Then I looked at where our expansion revenue was actually coming from."

A keeps two-line data setup + rhetorical question (different roles, different reveal). B abandons the format entirely, delivers same psychological move ("the cheap role is the important one") through retrospective confession.

### Example 2 — Three-sentence escalation
Source: "Google sent me a cease and desist at 13 years old. I had 2.5M users for my previous app. And my parents had no idea."
Mechanic: three short sentences, each escalating — external authority → personal scale → hidden context. Condensed movie trailer.

Version A: "A major customer threatened to sue us when I was 26. I had 40,000 businesses using the product. And my co-founder had no idea I'd been the one making the promises."
Version B: "The first time I got sued, the envelope was handed to me by my own lead engineer. He didn't know we were in breach of contract. I hadn't told anyone on the team."

A keeps three-sentence escalation. B uses two beats; hidden-context payload lands as closer, not setup. Same psychological move — authority-pressure-plus-secrecy — different rhythm.

### Example 3 — Analogy with deliberate pause
Source: "A 'data strategy' that starts with picking tools… … is like a fitness plan that starts with buying a smartwatch. Sure, the gear is shiny. But you haven't broken a sweat yet."
Mechanic: analogy where ellipsis is a pause beat. Four-beat tempo: setup, analogy, concession, real critique. Pacing is part of the mechanic.

Version A: "A 'GTM strategy' that starts with hiring a VP of Sales… … is like writing a book by buying a nice pen. Sure, the tool is expensive. But you haven't put a word down yet."
Version B: "Every founder I meet wants to talk about their tech stack. Almost none of them can tell me, in one sentence, why their top three customers bought from them and not from a competitor."

A preserves ellipsis-as-beat + four-beat tempo. B drops analogy entirely, delivers same "you're confusing tools with thinking" critique through direct observation.

You are an Opening Line Amplifier for B2B founder LinkedIn posts. Diagnose this post's opener through 7 gates, scan the body for buried gold, and generate exactly 5 alternative opening lines (Variants A through E).

## POST TEXT
We had five SE leads in three years. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same — the next hire will fix it. Better pedigree, stronger playbook, more enterprise logos on the resume.

Nobody asks why the role keeps failing.

In most cases the SE team wasn't the real problem. The timeline around the SE team was the problem. You can hire the best solutions leader in the world and give them eighteen months to fix what needs thirty-six. You load them with Series C expectations on a Series A foundation, then act surprised when they miss.

The board calls it a talent problem and authorizes another search.

But here's the part the industry is less comfortable saying. The SE leaders let this happen too. We trained them to say yes to impossible mandates, to accept the 'move fast' brief without asking what breaks in the process, to treat survival as success until the burnout becomes visible.

The ones who last got there by renegotiating the timeline before they took the chair — or by walking when they realized the negotiation was performative.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

## POST MODE (wrestling/declaring)
wrestling
